title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a high-severity heap out-of-bounds write in its MagicYUV decoder that JFrog used to achieve remote code execution against a Jellyfin media server through its normal library scan. Any application built on libavcodec is exposed, including Kodi, OBS Studio, Nextcloud, and desktop thumbnail generators, since a malicious AVI, MKV, or MOV file can trigger the flaw on open or preview.

title: Old and new apps, via modern coding agents
blurb: Terrence Tao used an AI coding agent to port two dozen of his defunct 1999 Java math applets to JavaScript in a matter of hours, restoring visualizations like a honeycomb tool that were previously non-functional. He found only one minor bug in the ported code, while the agent surfaced two bugs in his original work, and he also used it to finally build a special-relativity visualization tool he had abandoned decades ago.

title: Circle gets OCC approval to operate as a trust bank
blurb: Circle received OCC approval to operate as a national trust bank under the name Circle National Trust, letting it directly manage the cash and Treasury reserves backing its $73B USDC stablecoin rather than relying on third-party custodians. The charter, required for large issuers under the GENIUS Act, puts Circle under a single federal regulator and joins recent OCC moves from Coinbase, Ripple, and Paxos racing to own more of the regulated financial stack.

title: Inside the Unfair Judge: A Mechanistic Interpretability Account of LLM-as-Judge Bias
blurb: This paper reframes LLM-as-judge scoring bias as a feature of the model's internal geometry rather than input-output noise, showing across seven judges, seven bias types, and nine benchmarks that biased inputs occupy a distinct, low-dimensional activation subspace. Steering hidden states along that subspace induces or removes biased scores on demand, and a linear projection onto the same features predicts judge failures on unseen benchmarks better than text-based methods.

title: Lessons Learned from CISA’s Recent GitHub Leak
blurb: A CISA contractor left dozens of internal credentials, including administrative AWS GovCloud keys and a CSV of plaintext passwords for internal systems, in a public GitHub repository for nearly six months. CISA's postmortem flags two failures worth copying into any security playbook: key rotation took over 48 hours because secrets were poorly managed, and researchers had no clear channel to report the leak, forcing them through a contractor, a vulnerability disclosure form, and finally a journalist.

title: Quadrupling code performance with a "useless" if
blurb: Adding a seemingly useless `if` branch to a pointer-chasing loop quadrupled its throughput by breaking a serial dependency chain the CPU couldn't parallelize. The author shows that the `j = next_j[i][j]` lookup forced each iteration to wait on the previous one, and a branch that lets the processor speculate past the load restores instruction-level parallelism.

title: Researchers detail "context bombing", where defenders use prompt injections to trigger guardrails of attackers' LLMs, cutting AI hacking success rates by ~90% (Dan Goodin/Ars Technica)
blurb: Researchers detailed "context bombing," a defensive technique where targets seed their systems with prompt injections that trip the safety guardrails of an attacker's LLM. In tests, the approach cut the success rate of AI-driven hacking attempts by roughly 90%, turning the attacker's own model alignment into a liability against them.

title: Modernizing the Meta Ads Service With an Open-Source Kernel Scheduler
blurb: Meta rebuilt the CPU scheduler for its ads-serving fleet using sched_ext, the BPF-based extensible scheduling framework that landed in Linux kernel v6.12, after a general-purpose scheduler upgrade regressed latency. The workload-aware policy prioritizes threads that improve p99 request latency, cutting ads-retrieval tail latency 28%, saving 3.28 megawatts of power, and increasing the number of ads ranked by 1.1%.

title: Show HN: DOM-docx – HTML to native, editable Word docs (MIT)
blurb: DOM-docx is an MIT-licensed library that converts semantic HTML fragments into native, editable Word documents by mapping paragraphs, lists, tables, and images to OOXML rather than embedding screenshots or layout hacks. It runs in Node or fully in the browser with only docx, cheerio, and fflate as dependencies, and was tuned with a visual regression loop that renders HTML, converts to docx, and scores layout fidelity against a human-validated metric.

title: The US government warns that Russia state hackers are coming after your router
blurb: CISA and allied governments warned that Russia's FSB Center 16 hackers are mass-compromising poorly configured home and office routers via default SNMP credentials.

title: Grok Build Uploads Entire Git Repositories to xAI Storage, Not Just Files It Reads
blurb: A researcher testing xAI's Grok Build coding CLI found the agent uploaded entire Git repositories, full commit history included, to an xAI-run Google Cloud Storage bucket, not just the files a task needed. On a 12 GB repo the model never read, storage traffic moved 5.10 GiB while model-turn traffic was 192 KB, and disabling "Improve the model" did nothing to stop it.

title: Don't you mean extinct?
blurb: In 1993, Jurassic Park's photorealistic CGI dinosaurs blindsided stop-motion master Phil Tippett, who told Spielberg he felt "extinct" as decades of go-motion expertise became obsolete overnight. The author uses the anecdote to reframe programmer anxiety over LLMs: every generation faces a similar reinvention, from the early web to mobile-first, and the way to avoid extinction is to learn the new tool rather than resist it.

title: Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API, benchmarked against Whisper and its predecessor
blurb: Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API, introduced in iOS 26 and macOS 26 to replace SFSpeechRecognizer, posted the lowest word error rate of any on-device English engine in Inscribe's benchmark, beating every Whisper model it ships. It cut error rates 3.5 to 4x over the legacy API and beat Whisper Small on both clean and noisy LibriSpeech splits while running roughly three times faster.

title: Modern Data Warehousing at Scale: Arcesium's Migration to DuckDB and Iceberg
blurb: Arcesium replaced its legacy data warehouse with a stack built on DuckDB for query execution and Apache Iceberg for table storage. The engineering team details how the combination handles large-scale analytical workloads while cutting infrastructure cost and complexity, using DuckDB's in-process engine to run queries directly against Iceberg tables in object storage.

title: Introducing Precursor: detecting agentic behavior with continuous client-side signals
blurb: Cloudflare launched Precursor, a client-side verification system that injects JavaScript to continuously collect behavioral signals across an entire session, distinguishing human traffic from automated or agentic bots in real time. Available as an optional complement to Turnstile within Enterprise Bot Management, it targets modern automation that can execute JavaScript and pass individual CAPTCHAs but struggles to mimic consistent human behavior over time.

title: New York becomes the first state to enact a data center moratorium
blurb: New York became the first state to enact a data center moratorium, with Governor Kathy Hochul signing an executive order that blocks new environmental permits for data centers over 50 megawatts for up to a year. The pause gives the state time to draft rules on energy prices, water use, and air quality, while a stricter legislative bill with a 20-megawatt threshold still awaits Hochul's signature.

title: Europe's Anduril rival Helsing raises $1.8 billion at $18 billion valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation, making the Munich-based defense startup one of Europe's most valuable, with JPMorgan, Lightspeed, and Iconiq among the backers. The company builds AI-driven drones and underwater surveillance systems, including the HX-2 drones supplied to Ukraine, and positions itself as Europe's answer to Anduril, which raised $5B at a $61B valuation in May.

title: European defencetech leader Helsing secures $1.8B Series E at $18B valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation, with new and existing investors including Lightspeed, Iconiq, and CPP Investments after demand exceeded the allocation. Founded in 2021, the European defence AI company builds software that fuses drone, radar, and satellite data into a real-time operational picture and has expanded into strike drones and underwater surveillance systems.

title: Anthropic research based on ~310K anonymized Claude conversations shows how Claude's expressed values and behaviors vary across models and languages (Jason Nelson/Decrypt)
blurb: Anthropic analyzed roughly 310K anonymized Claude conversations to measure how the model's expressed values and behaviors shift across different Claude versions and user languages. The study found meaningful variation in traits like helpfulness and caution depending on the language a user writes in, suggesting alignment work does not transfer uniformly across a model's full range of interactions.

title: Google and Microsoft Pull ModHeader With 1.6 Million Installs After Dormant Collector Found
blurb: Google and Microsoft pulled ModHeader, a header-editing extension with 1.6M installs, after researchers found a dormant browsing-history collector in its official build.

title: Now, defenders are embracing the prompt injection, too
blurb: Researchers at Tracebit found that planting prompt injections next to fake AWS secrets, a technique they call context bombing, reliably shuts down AI hacking agents by tripping the models' own refusal guardrails. Across 152 runs on five leading models, the decoy strings cut full-admin compromise from 57% to 5%, with Opus 4.8 dropping from admin access in 93% of runs to zero.

title: Helsing’s $18B valuation is not really about Helsing — private markets are pricing European rearmament as a multi-decade procurement cycle, and the capital is positioning accordingly
blurb: Helsing raised a $1.8B Series E at an $18B valuation, an oversubscribed round backing the Munich-based company's software that fuses drone, radar, satellite, and camera data into a single battlefield picture and its move into autonomous strike drones. The cap table signals a broader shift: a Canadian pension fund, JPMorgan, Goldman's alternatives arm, and Lightspeed are now underwriting European defence tech that ESG mandates recently kept off-limits.

title: Unauthenticated RCE in Motorola's MR2600 Router
blurb: A researcher found an unauthenticated RCE in Motorola's MR2600 router, where a botched SEAMA magic-byte check lets attackers upload malicious firmware unauthenticated.

title: Linux disk encryption and self-encrypting drives -- A case study on Opal2 drives security
blurb: Researchers black-box tested 38 commercial Opal2 self-encrypting drives on Linux and found several firmware security issues and vendor incompatibilities, which they responsibly disclosed. The work led to fixes in the disk encryption tools shipped across all major Linux distributions, and the team released their Opal2 test scenarios as an open-source toolset for independent evaluation.

title: Apple releases the first public betas of iOS 27, iPadOS 27, macOS 27 Golden Gate, watchOS 27, and tvOS 27, marking the new Siri AI's first public launch (Will Shanklin/Engadget)
blurb: Apple released the first public betas of iOS 27, iPadOS 27, macOS 27 Golden Gate, watchOS 27, and tvOS 27, marking the first public launch of its overhauled Siri AI. The betas open the redesigned assistant to testers ahead of the fall release, giving Apple its widest real-world trial yet as it races to close the gap with rivals' voice and agent features.

title: Is x86 ready to ACE it?
blurb: The x86 Ecosystem Advisory Group published a whitepaper and specification for ACE, a new accelerator type that extends Intel's AMX matrix-multiplication framework. ACE drops AMX's configurable tile registers in favor of fixed 64-byte by 16-row tiles, swaps complex-number support for FP8, and uses outer-product instructions instead of inner-product ones, positioning x86 against Arm's Scalable Matrix Extension for low-latency machine learning on the CPU.

title: Since Chromium 148, Math.tanh is now fingerprintable to link underlying OS
blurb: Since Chromium 148, V8 computes Math.tanh through the host system's math library instead of its bundled fdlibm port, so the last bits of the result now vary by operating system. Because glibc, Apple's libsystem_m, and Windows UCRT round transcendental functions differently, a single call like Math.tanh(0.8) betrays the real OS, letting anti-bot systems catch a browser whose math contradicts its User-Agent.

title: The Economics of Recursive Self-Improvement [pdf]
blurb: This paper models recursive self-improvement as an economic process, arguing that the pace of AI systems improving themselves is bounded by compute costs, diminishing returns on each iteration, and the price of the resources each gain requires. The authors conclude that runaway "intelligence explosion" scenarios are constrained by these economics, making gradual, capital-limited progress the more likely path than a sudden takeoff.

title: Singapore-based and Alibaba-backed video generation startup PixVerse says it closed a Series C extension to raise $439M total for the round, valuing it at $2B+ (Ivan Mehta/TechCrunch)
blurb: PixVerse closed a Series C extension that brings the round to $439M total, valuing the Alibaba-backed video generation startup at more than $2B. The Singapore-based company competes with OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo in AI video, and the fresh capital signals continued investor appetite for consumer-facing generative video tools.

title: Sources: the Trump admin and industry groups discuss streamlining the releases of US open models of equal or lesser capability than leading Chinese open models (Benjamin Guggenheim/Washington Post)
blurb: The Trump administration is discussing with industry groups how to streamline the release of US open-weight models that match or trail the capabilities of leading Chinese open models, per the Washington Post. The talks aim to speed government review of open releases as Chinese labs like DeepSeek and Alibaba set the pace, framing open-model competition as a national priority.

title: HTTP gets a QUERY method so complex searches can stop pretending to be POST
blurb: The IETF standardized an HTTP QUERY method, letting complex searches carry a request body safely instead of overloading POST or bloating URLs with GET.

title: Figma Acquires Team Behind a Vibe-coding App
blurb: Figma acquired the team behind Bud, the AI agent and vibe-coding platform formerly known as Orchids, to bring app building and prototyping closer to its design canvas. Both products shut down by July 18, forcing users to migrate projects, as Figma pushes past static design with tools like Figma Make and integrations with Codex and Claude Code.

title: EU sanctions Russian GRU military hackers over cyberattacks
blurb: The EU and UK issued their first joint cyber sanctions package, targeting dozens of Russian GRU and FSB hackers behind attacks across Europe.

title: The art and engineering of Sega CD Silpheed
blurb: Fabien Sanglard reverse engineered the full-motion video format behind Silpheed, the 1993 Sega CD shooter whose near-fullscreen cutscenes ran on a 12.5MHz 68000 CPU with 16 colors and just 8 KiB per frame. Notably, he did the two-week project without writing a line of code himself, relying instead on an AI framework he plans to open-source and describes as a genuinely pleasant way to work.

title: Improving Smart Tiered Cache for public cloud regions
blurb: Cloudflare shipped Smart Tiered Cache for Public Cloud Regions, which lets you supply a cloud region hint so Cloudflare can map anycast or regional-unicast origins to the right region and pick better primary and fallback upper tiers. The original 2021 feature couldn't lock latency probes onto public cloud origins that look equally close to a dozen data centers, so it fell back to multiple tiers and lost the cache efficiency of a single closest tier.

title: Helsing raises $1.8bn backed by Goldman and Lightspeed
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in a Series E backed by Goldman Sachs Alternatives, Lightspeed, JPMorganChase, Dragoneer, and Iconiq, marking one of the largest funding rounds ever for a European startup. The Munich defence firm, which builds battlefield AI, autonomous strike drones, and underwater surveillance gliders, will use the capital to expand AI platforms for European and allied armed forces amid rising defence budgets.

title: Chinese AI Startup DFSX Releases Chip to Take on the West
blurb: Chinese startup Dongfang Suanxin (DFSX) released a self-designed AI chip built on a fully domestic supply chain, sidestepping US restrictions on high-end semiconductor technology. The launch adds to Beijing's push for chip self-sufficiency, aiming to give Chinese firms a homegrown alternative to Nvidia hardware that export controls have put out of reach.

title: Sources: Nvidia intensified due diligence and cut its authorized AI chip customers in Singapore, Malaysia, and Japan by 50%+ to prevent China diversions (Zijing Wu/Financial Times)
blurb: Nvidia sharply tightened export controls, cutting its authorized AI chip customers in Singapore, Malaysia, and Japan by more than 50% and intensifying due diligence to block hardware from being rerouted to China. The move reflects mounting US pressure on Nvidia to police its supply chain, as regulators scrutinize whether restricted chips are reaching Chinese buyers through third-country intermediaries.

title: Deluxe Corporation beats the odds with mainframe migration using AI
blurb: Deluxe Corporation migrated off a 50-plus-year-old mainframe to the cloud in about 12 months, using AI to rewrite legacy code, generate documentation, and produce test cases. The move will save roughly $4.9M a year and bucks a Gartner forecast that over 70% of 2026 mainframe exit projects will fail from overreliance on generative AI.

title: Elon Musk says SpaceXAI will delete all Grok Build user data following privacy concerns
blurb: Elon Musk said SpaceXAI will delete all Grok Build user data after security researchers found the tool uploaded entire Git repositories, including private code, commit histories, and API keys, to Google Cloud. The incident underscores how coding agents that ingest whole repos expand the attack surface for credential leaks, putting the burden on vendors to prove what they collect and where it goes.

title: The git history command
blurb: Git 2.55 completes the new `git history` command, an experimental built-in for editing past commits that adds fixup, reword, and split subcommands across the 2.54 and 2.55 releases. Every operation is atomic and refuses to run if it would create a conflict, and fixup rewrites all local branches descended from the target commit, delivering some of jj's rebase ergonomics without leaving core git.

title: TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, reports 68% surge in June revenue
blurb: TSMC reported a 67.9% year-over-year jump in June revenue, pushing first-half 2026 sales to NT$2.4 trillion ($75 billion), up 35.6% from a year earlier. The world's largest contract chipmaker is sold out on its N3 process used by Nvidia, Apple, and AMD, and is on track for over $40 billion in AI chip revenue this year, roughly 25% of its total.

title: Samsung Is Said in Early Discussions on Potential US Listing
blurb: Samsung Electronics is in early discussions on a potential US listing through American depositary receipts, according to people familiar with the matter. An ADR offering would broaden the chipmaker's access to US investors amid heavy AI-driven demand for its memory and foundry business, though the talks remain preliminary and no decision has been made.

title: Control the Ideas, Not the Code
blurb: antirez argues that once you control the ideas behind your software, reviewing the code line by line is suboptimal, since LLMs generate more code than anyone can meaningfully audit and are strongest at locally optimal implementation rather than big-picture design. He frames this not as vibe coding toward a final product, but as directing the design and interrogating how each part works, treating code as an output rather than the primary object of attention.

title: Nvidia Partner GMI Cloud Seeks $635 Million GPU-Backed Bank Loan
blurb: GMI Cloud, a US-based data center operator and Nvidia partner, is seeking a NT$20.45 billion ($635 million) multi-tranche bank loan backed by its customer GPU contracts. The deal is one of the first GPU-collateralized financings in Asia, reflecting how regional AI demand is pushing operators toward novel debt structures secured by compute commitments rather than traditional assets.

title: New York Set to Temporarily Ban Large New Data Centers
blurb: New York is set to temporarily ban construction of large new data centers, with Gov. Kathy Hochul pausing approvals for up to a year while the state drafts regulations. The moratorium targets the environmental and electricity-grid strain of AI-driven buildout, making New York one of the first states to formally slow data center growth over energy concerns.

title: Automation Without Understanding
blurb: This essay argues that AI systems now producing research-level mathematics, coupled with weakened US support for training mathematicians, constitutes a strategic error that erodes the human capacity to verify and challenge machine reasoning. Citing the May 2026 AI disproof of an Erdős conjecture, the author treats mathematical capability as infrastructure on par with semiconductors and proposes requiring AI to expose decision-critical claims in formal, machine-checkable form.

title: Drohnen-Startup Helsing ist jetzt mehr wert als die Lufthansa: 18 Milliarden
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation, making the Munich defense-AI startup Germany's most valuable startup and worth more than Lufthansa's roughly $12B market cap. The company builds AI software that makes drones, aircraft, and other weapons systems more precise, and it plans to use the funding to develop new AI platforms and integrate them into partner countries' defense capabilities.

title: Meta expands colossal Hyperion AI supercluster plans to 5GW, pushes Louisiana investment past $50 billion as AI race accelerates — says it plans to invest over $1 billion in local infrastructure improvements
blurb: Meta will expand its Hyperion data center in Richland Parish, Louisiana, to 5GW of compute capacity from an initial 2GW, pushing its planned regional investment past $50 billion. The company also committed over $1 billion to local roads, water, and wastewater systems, and its Entergy Louisiana deal adds 5.2GW of natural-gas capacity plus support for up to 2.5GW of new solar.

title: Networking and the Internet, from First Principles
blurb: This article explains how the internet works from first principles, following data from radio waves and copper to light pulses in undersea fiber and back. The author argues that packet switching, TCP, DNS, and TLS were each patches for a specific problem deployed long after the internet already worked, and building the model this way makes everyday mysteries like the address-bar padlock intuitive.

title: Meta's Louisiana data center investment to reach $50 billion, aided by generous tax incentives
blurb: Meta will raise its Hyperion data center investment in Richland Parish, Louisiana to more than $50 billion, nearly doubling the $27 billion figure from October as the facility expands to 5 GW. The buildout leans on a 20-year state sales tax exemption for data centers, part of a broader competition among states offering tax rebates and energy deals to court hyperscalers chasing AI capacity.

title: YouTrackDB is a general-use object-oriented graph database
blurb: JetBrains open-sourced YouTrackDB, a general-purpose object-oriented graph database it runs internally in production, built around O(1) link traversal that avoids runtime JOINs. It supports the TinkerPop API and Gremlin alongside its own SQL-based YQL query language, runs transactions under snapshot isolation by default, and works in schema-less, schema-mixed, or schema-full modes.

title: Apple’s ‘Thermonuclear’ Response to the OpenAI Threat
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI under Tim Cook, reviving the litigation-as-defense playbook Steve Jobs once aimed at Android. The bet is that legal delay can slow a rival threatening to upend the iPhone era, though the WSJ argues courtroom wins rarely stop a platform shift already underway.

title: Video-generation startup PixVerse raises $439M, valuation soars past $2B
blurb: PixVerse, a Singapore-based video-generation startup, raised $439M in a Series C extension that pushes its valuation past $2B, with backing from Alibaba, Mirae Asset, and returning investors. The company will use the funding to expand its world model offerings and grow internationally, building on a consumer product with over 150 million registered users and models that generate 4K video with baked-in audio.

title: Trillion Dollar Chip Rout Trains Spotlight on TSMC and ASML Results
blurb: TSMC and ASML report earnings this week against a backdrop of a trillion-dollar selloff in AI-linked chip stocks, making the two suppliers' guidance a bellwether for the entire trade. Investors will read TSMC's capacity outlook and ASML's order book as the clearest signal of whether AI hardware demand still justifies valuations that have driven the recent runup.

title: Grok CLI uploaded the whole home directory to GCS
blurb: A viral post claims xAI's Grok CLI coding tool uploaded a user's entire home directory to Google Cloud Storage, including SSH keys, a password manager database, and personal files. The incident, which reached 405 points on Hacker News, renews scrutiny of how much filesystem access agentic coding tools take by default and how easily an autonomous agent can exfiltrate sensitive data.

title: Tiny Emulators
blurb: Tiny Emulators is a browser-based collection of cycle-accurate 8-bit machine emulators, covering hardware like the Commodore C64, ZX Spectrum, Amstrad CPC, and the East German KC85 and Robotron lines. Each system runs directly in WebAssembly with a debugger UI, and ships with a large library of period games, demos, and language environments including FORTH, BASIC, and CP/M.

title: I love LLMs, I hate hype
blurb: George Hotz argues that AI progress is real and exciting but decoupled from the doom-and-FOMO hype pushed by frontier labs, whose valuations rest on capturing value they won't actually own. He contends AI is advancing mostly due to Moore's law and general computing progress, so the core case against open source is fear of commodification rather than genuine safety or geopolitical concerns.

title: Muse Spark 1.1
blurb: Meta Superintelligence Labs released Muse Spark 1.1, a multimodal reasoning model built for agentic tasks like planning, tool use, and computer control across apps, with a context window up to 1 million tokens. It is available now in Thinking mode inside the Meta AI app and via a new OpenAI-compatible Meta Model API in public preview, positioned to run real coding workloads at scale.

title: Apple’s M6, M7 and M8 Chips Show How AI Is Reshaping the Company
blurb: Bloomberg reports that Apple is planning a chip roadmap spanning the M6, M7 Pro, M7 Max, M7 Ultra, and M8, with the designs increasingly shaped by the demands of on-device AI. The report also details upcoming touchscreen MacBook Pros and new Apple Pencils, pointing to a broad hardware refresh as Apple reorganizes its silicon strategy around machine learning workloads.

title: Brazil’s First Ethanol-Powered Ship Sails in Win for Biofuels
blurb: Brazil sent its first ethanol-powered container ship out from the port of Santos, the first vessel to run on domestically produced ethanol fuel. The test targets emissions from maritime shipping, one of the harder sectors to decarbonize, and stakes an early claim for biofuels as an alternative to the liquefied natural gas and methanol paths favored elsewhere.

title: Meta Files Patent for AI That Can Listen All Day and Track How You're Feeling
blurb: Meta filed a patent application for AI that continuously listens to a user's voice, infers emotional state from tone, pace, sighs, and word choice, and keeps a timestamped log tied to location and activity. Filed in December 2025 and published July 2 as US 2026/0182881, the system spans smart glasses, phones, watches, and speakers, and can fold in eye-tracking and biometric signals. Meta has not announced a product.

title: Apple Sues OpenAI, Apple’s Real Problem
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI over the alleged theft of trade secrets, but the case traces to a single guilty employee and reads mostly as frustration rather than a substantive claim. The author argues the lawsuit exposes Apple's deeper problem, which is that it has fallen behind in AI and is lashing out at a rival it cannot match on product.

title: Linux 0.11 rewritten in idiomatic Rust, boots in QEMU
blurb: linux-0.11-rs rewrites the 1991 Linux 0.11 kernel from scratch in idiomatic Rust, preserving the original's semantics while adding stronger types, clearer module boundaries, and a std-shaped user library, and it boots on emulated i386 in QEMU. The project ships processes, demand-paged virtual memory with CoW fork, the Minix v1 filesystem, an 80+ command coreutils userland, a POSIX-subset shell, and a one-command script that builds a bootable disk image.

title: Uber’s product chief on hotels, robotaxis, and why the company doesn’t want to be “everything for everyone”
blurb: Uber Chief Product Officer Sachin Kansal told TechCrunch the company is expanding into hotels, boat rentals, shopping concierge features, and driver debit cards, while insisting it won't chase the Asian super-app model of being "everything for everyone." Kansal also detailed AV Labs, a six-month-old unit building a sensor-equipped fleet to collect driving data, giving Uber leverage and a hedge against autonomous partners like Waymo that it both invests in and competes with.

title: European "age verification" "app" forcing everyone to use Android or iOS
blurb: A GitHub discussion on the EU Digital Identity Wallet's age-verification spec has drawn nearly 600 comments objecting to its reliance on Google Play Integrity and Apple's equivalent attestation, which would require users to run a Google- or Apple-blessed OS to prove their age online. Critics argue the design locks out de-Googled Android builds, custom ROMs, and open-source clients, turning a privacy measure into a mandate for platform gatekeepers.

title: LAPD lets contract with surveillance giant Flock expire, citing ‘serious concerns’ over civil liberties and privacy
blurb: The LAPD, the third-largest police department in the US and one of Flock Safety's biggest government customers, is letting its three-year contract with the license-plate-tracking company expire over "serious concerns" about civil liberties and privacy. Several other cities including Mountain View and South Portland have also dropped Flock, some citing federal immigration agents using its 80,000-camera network to track people in sanctuary jurisdictions.

title: Apple says former employee exploited ‘rare’ bug to download confidential files after leaving for OpenAI
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI for trade-secret theft, alleging a former electrical engineer named Chang Liu exploited a zero-day authentication bug to siphon dozens of confidential hardware files from Apple's network weeks after leaving for OpenAI. The files allegedly covered unreleased products, engineering presentations, and technical specifications, and Apple says server logs show Liu accessed them using an unreturned work laptop after his employment ended.

title: Uber’s robotaxi lobbying effort puts it on a collision course with Waymo
blurb: Uber is lobbying against a Washington, D.C. bill that would let robotaxis operate without human drivers, putting it at odds with its partner and investor target Waymo. Uber argues the rule hands Alphabet-owned Waymo a de facto monopoly and displaces for-hire drivers, and is pushing a hybrid model that requires autonomous fleets to run on ride-hailing networks that also use humans. The two will testify at a D.C. Council hearing Monday.

title: Apple’s public betas for iOS 27 and more are out now
blurb: Apple released public betas for iOS 27, iPadOS 27, watchOS 27, and macOS 27 Golden Gate ahead of a wider launch this fall. The headline addition is the delayed AI-powered Siri revamp, which testers can try early, though Apple warns of glitches and faster battery drain on beta builds.

title: Investors send General Fusion soaring in debut as first publicly traded fusion company
blurb: General Fusion began trading on the Nasdaq under the ticker GFUZ, becoming the first publicly listed fusion power company after completing a SPAC merger with Spring Valley Acquisition Corp. III. The stock rose 40% from $12.85 on its debut, but heavy redemptions left the cash-strapped firm with only about $150M, well short of the $230M the deal could have delivered.

title: In the wake of China's "embodied AI" push, there's an urgency among China's 100+ humanoid startups to launch IPOs; LimX Dynamics raises $200M in a pre-IPO round (Evelyn Cheng/CNBC)
blurb: LimX Dynamics raised $200M in a pre-IPO round as China's humanoid robot makers rush toward public listings. The funding reflects broader urgency among China's 100+ humanoid startups to capitalize on Beijing's "embodied AI" push, with many racing to file IPOs before the sector consolidates.

title: OpenAI’s Feud With xAI Carries On as Apple Secrets Fight Revs Up
blurb: OpenAI asked a judge to dismiss xAI's trade secret theft lawsuit, arguing the case "should never have been filed" and seeking to recover more than $1M in legal expenses from Elon Musk's company. The motion escalates the ongoing feud between the two labs, unfolding alongside a separate fight over allegations involving Apple secrets.

title: While Musk's Neuralink drills into skulls, China's BrainCo bets the future of brain tech is wearable
blurb: China's BrainCo is betting that mass-market brain-computer interfaces won't require opening the skull, building wearable BCI prosthetics and devices as a non-invasive alternative to Elon Musk's implant-based Neuralink. The bet reflects a widening US-China rivalry in which Beijing named BCI a strategic "future industry," though most experts consider human augmentation, the field's largest potential market, still closer to science fiction than product.

title: SpaceX is gearing up for Starship's 13th test flight later this week
blurb: SpaceX plans to launch Starship's 13th test flight as soon as Thursday, the second flight of its newest ship design. The mission will carry 20 real Starlink V3 satellites for the first time, deploying them from an onboard dispenser to test laser links with satellites already in low-Earth orbit before Starship splashes down northwest of Australia.

title: Pure DC Building Finland AI Data Center, Secures Microsoft Deal
blurb: Pure Data Centres, the Oaktree Capital-backed UK firm, is building an AI data center in Finland that Microsoft and other clients are slated to use. The project is part of a broader push to expand European digital infrastructure as demand grows to train and run AI systems on the continent rather than route workloads through overseas capacity.

title: Europe Takes Step Toward Possible Social Media Ban for Children
blurb: The European Commission is considering a bloc-wide social media age restriction for children after a new report on the risks to minors. Any rule change would apply across all 27 member states, adding regulatory pressure on platforms already facing age-verification mandates in individual European countries.

title: Huawei's $11 Billion Clean Energy Empire Is Opening New Markets
blurb: Huawei's energy unit generated $11B in revenue last year, approaching the scale of Tesla's energy division as it expands solar, storage, and grid infrastructure globally. Known primarily for telecom equipment, the Chinese company has become a major supplier in the energy transition, using the business to open new markets amid US sanctions on its core operations.

title: Q&A with Xinzhou Wu, head of automotive at Nvidia, on Nvidia's chips and AI models for autonomous driving, lidar's usefulness for Level 4 autonomy, and more (Nilay Patel/The Verge)
blurb: Nvidia automotive head Xinzhou Wu laid out how the company's chips and AI models are being built to run Level 4 autonomous driving stacks. In the interview, Wu defends lidar as a necessary sensor for higher-autonomy vehicles, arguing that redundancy across cameras, radar, and lidar matters more for safety than the camera-only approach favored by some rivals.

title: OpenAI Is Showing Kalshi’s World Cup Odds in ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI partnered with prediction market Kalshi to display live World Cup odds in ChatGPT, its first data partnership of this kind. The deal pipes Kalshi's real-time market data into some ChatGPT search results about the soccer tournament, giving OpenAI a source of structured, up-to-the-minute information beyond its training data.

title: Meta boosts investment in Hyperion data center campus to $50B+
blurb: Meta raised its planned investment in the Hyperion data center campus in Richland Parish, Louisiana to more than $50B, up from a $27B budget last year and an initial $10B in 2024. The site will deliver over 5 gigawatts of compute powered by 10 new Entergy plants, and reportedly reaches $250B once Nvidia GPUs and Meta's custom MTI 500 inference chips are counted.

title: Thomson Reuters is cutting engineers and hiring AI-native ones
blurb: Thomson Reuters is cutting up to 500 engineering jobs globally, roughly 5.2% of its 9,400-person operations and technology division, as it pushes AI through its legal, tax, and regulatory products. The company plans to hire more than 250 net-new engineering roles over the next two years, "the large majority senior and AI-native," part of a broader industry pattern that has eliminated about 120,000 tech jobs across 228 companies in 2026.

title: Defense technology startup Helsing raises $1.8B at $18B valuation
blurb: Helsing, the German defense technology startup, raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation from 10 investors including JPMorgan Chase, General Catalyst, Lightspeed, and Iconiq. The company builds autonomous military hardware and AI, including the CA-1 Europa jet drone and its Centaur flight-control software, which uses reinforcement learning and has flown a fighter jet in Baltic Sea tests.

title: Australia finds serious gaps in Big Tech’s response to child sexual abuse online
blurb: Australia's eSafety regulator told Apple, Meta, and Google that they have significant gaps in detecting child sexual exploitation, including a failure to deploy language analysis that flags the scripted phrases sextortion offenders reuse across thousands of approaches. The transparency report, drawn from over 2,000 sextortion complaints in the second half of 2025, also found inconsistent livestream-abuse detection and reporting tools on WhatsApp, iMessage, and Discord that often lack a category for the abuse at all.

title: A new sodium battery posts wild four-minute charging numbers, but don’t expect it in an EV yet
blurb: Researchers reported a sodium-metal battery that charged in roughly four minutes at a 15C rate in a small experimental cell, using a new quasi-solid gel electrolyte called Sn-FB QSE that suppresses the dendrites which usually short these cells. The headline speed came from lab-scale cells, not the pouch-cell prototypes, which retained only 60% to 84% capacity over 19 to 100 cycles, keeping real EV use years away.

title: 1X Technologies designs humanoid hand to "match or surpass human capability"
blurb: 1X Technologies redesigned the hands on its Neo humanoid robot to match or surpass human dexterity, aiming to let the robot perform virtually any manual task a person can. The updated hands will ship on the first consumer Neo model, a 5-foot-6-inch home robot the company plans to deliver to households this year.

title: Apple's AI struggle turns ugly as it sues OpenAI and io, the company founded by iPhone designer Jony Ive
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI and io Products, the hardware startup founded by former Apple design chief Jony Ive, accusing the company of a "pattern of theft" including hiring ex-Apple staff who allegedly kept accessing confidential hardware files. The suit deepens a rift that saw Apple drop ChatGPT for Google's Gemini in Siri earlier this year, as both companies now race toward competing AI hardware.

title: OpenAI hardware timeline reportedly unchanged after Apple trade secret theft lawsuit
blurb: OpenAI still plans to announce its first hardware product this year and ship it in 2027, Bloomberg reports, even after Apple sued the company for allegedly stealing trade secrets through former Apple employees now working on its devices. It is OpenAI's second hardware-related trade secret suit in about a year, following a case from startup iyO, though the company says its plans could shift as it digests Apple's claims.

title: Apple lawsuit reveals how many of its former employees now work at OpenAI
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI on Friday for trade secret theft, and the filing reveals that more than 400 former Apple employees now work at the company. Apple argues that OpenAI exploited that knowledge to jumpstart its hardware efforts, poaching engineers like former Vision Pro lead Paul Meade and design chief Tang Tan to structure interviews that solicited additional confidential information.

title: FCC approves orbital space mirrors, first test satellites will launch this year — large spacecraft reflects sunlight to Earth’s surface for construction sites, search-and-rescue lighting, and more
blurb: The FCC approved Reflect Orbital's Eärendil-1 satellite, which carries four 18-meter thin-film reflectors designed to bounce sunlight onto specific spots on Earth to extend daylight for construction sites, search-and-rescue operations, and solar farms. The California startup plans two test launches this year and up to 50,000 satellites by 2035, though astronomers including the Vera C. Rubin Observatory's chief scientist warn the mirrors would disrupt night-sky observations more severely than Starlink.

title: Researchers create programmable material that can steer heat and remember its state without power — breakthrough could eventually aid AI chip cooling and silicon photonics
blurb: Researchers at Osaka Metropolitan University built a programmable thermal device that steers where heat radiates and retains its configuration after power is cut. Combining a magneto-optical layer with germanium-antimony-tellurium phase-change material, it breaks Kirchhoff's law to control absorption and emission independently, which could sharpen thermal management in AI chips, silicon photonics, and infrared sensors.

title: Micron commits $500 million to GlobalWafers' Texas wafer plant as it raises U.S. spending to $250 billion — memory maker aims to manufacture 40% of DRAM in the US by 2035
blurb: Micron committed up to $3B to shore up its US semiconductor supply chain, including $500M in strategic financing for GlobalWafers' Sherman, Texas plant, the only domestic source of advanced 300mm wafers its fabs depend on. The company also raised planned US spending to more than $250B through 2035, tied to a goal of manufacturing 40% of its DRAM domestically by the mid-2030s.

title: Intel's new space-grade Starfire chip is a Panther Lake SoC that puts an 18A CPU into orbit — chip designed for the US government leverages Intel 3 for the GPU
blurb: Intel introduced Starfire, a space-grade Panther Lake SoC for the U.S. government that pairs eight CPU cores and a three-tile NPU on its 18A node with an Intel 3 graphics tile, reaching up to 75 TOPS across two 10 W and 35 W SKUs. The chip targets on-orbit AI inference, a bracket beyond the RAD750 that has flown spacecraft for two decades, though Intel lists it as not yet radiation-qualified with samples due in Q3.

title: Apple acquires observability startup SigScalr
blurb: Apple acquired certain assets of SigScalr and hired its team, the startup behind SigLens, an open-source observability platform for collecting and analyzing logs, metrics, and traces. Positioned as a leaner alternative to Splunk, Datadog, and Elasticsearch, SigLens has been archived and made read-only since the March 12 deal, pointing to internal use across Apple's infrastructure rather than a consumer product.

title: EU Commission preliminarily finds Meta apps addictive
blurb: The EU Commission preliminarily found that Instagram and Facebook breach the Digital Services Act, ruling that design features like infinite scroll and autoplay create addictive behavioral effects, especially in minors. Meta now faces potential fines of up to 6% of global annual revenue if the findings are confirmed, adding to the company's mounting regulatory pressure in Europe.

title: Kode Dot Programmable pocket device for makers, pentesters and geeks
blurb: Programmable ESP32-P4 pocket device with AMOLED touchscreen, NFC, IR, and GPIO for makers and pentesters.

title: Apple claims OpenAI staff exploited network vulnerability to siphon trade secrets
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI and former employees, alleging an ex-Apple engineer who joined OpenAI's hardware division exploited an authentication bug to download over a thousand pages of confidential hardware files. The suit names OpenAI hardware chief Tang Yew Tan and Jony Ive's io Products as defendants, framing the alleged trade-secret theft as behavior "normalized and exemplified by leadership.

title: The shingles vaccine may reduce the risk of dementia
blurb: A large study found that the shingles vaccine measurably lowers the risk of developing dementia, adding to evidence that some viral infections may drive neurodegeneration. The correlation strengthens the case for viral triggers behind conditions like Alzheimer's, suggesting that widespread vaccination could double as a low-cost tool for protecting long-term brain health.

title: AI boosts research careers but narrow the span of ideas explored: study
blurb: A study of over 40 million academic papers found that scientists who use AI tools publish more, earn more citations, and reach leadership roles faster than peers who don't. The same work shows AI-heavy research clusters around the same data-rich problems and covers less topical ground, exposing a tension between individual career gains and the collective narrowing of what science explores.

title: OpenAI's head of safety is reportedly leaving as part of company reorganization
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety systems, Johannes Heidecke, is reportedly leaving as part of a restructuring that folds the safety teams into research under new VP Mia Glaese, according to Wired. Saachi Jain will serve as interim head of safety systems, and chief research officer Mark Chen framed the move as integrating safety work earlier into frontier-model development and launch decisions.

title: Japan develops a method to recover up to 90% of lithium from used EV batteries
blurb: Japanese researchers developed a lithium-recovery process that extracts up to 90% of the metal from used EV batteries, roughly double the sub-50% yield of conventional recycling. The method swaps standard sodium hydroxide for recovered lithium hydroxide to convert battery "black mass" into high-purity lithium, and researchers say it cuts recycling emissions by about 40% while reducing Japan's near-total reliance on imported battery minerals.

title: Benchmarking 15 “E-Waste” GPUs with Modern Workloads
blurb: A developer benchmarked 15 decommissioned NVIDIA Tesla enterprise GPUs, including the K80, P100, and V100, against modern LLM and video workloads to test whether cheap "e-waste" cards still hold up. The economics are the draw: a 24GB K80 sells for $60 and a 16GB V100 for under $200, and running slightly older software like a source-built llama.cpp keeps even 2014-era Kepler cards usable in a homelab.

title: Logseq 2.0 Beta (DB version) is here
blurb: Logseq released its first 2.0 beta, rebuilding the note-taking app on a database backend instead of the plain-text Markdown files the current version stores locally. The DB version is an early beta with rough edges, so users are advised to back up data before testing, with feedback shaping the eventual stable release.

title: EU plans to ban under-13s from social media, with ‘phased access’ afterwards
blurb: The EU proposed banning under-13s from social media without parental consent, followed by "phased and gradual access" for teens aged 13 to 16 as platforms prove their apps are age-appropriate. The plan joins a growing list of countries including Australia, the UK, and France imposing age limits, and follows an EU warning to Meta that infinite scrolling may already be illegal under existing law.

title: The White House’s next AI energy pledge targets the utilities
blurb: The White House is preparing an event within weeks where electric utilities, data center operators, and governors of states hosting major buildouts will be asked to pledge that AI power costs won't land on household electricity bills. The commitment extends a voluntary March pledge signed by Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, and xAI, but it remains non-binding, and utilities are planning roughly $1.4 trillion in capital spending by 2030 to serve AI demand.

title: The infinite scroll may become endangered if controversial Calif. law passes
blurb: A California bill from Assemblymember Josh Lowenthal would require Meta, Reddit, and other platforms to offer under-16 users a less addictive feed stripped of features like infinite scroll, or block them from creating accounts entirely. Companies would have until 2028 to comply, and an expert oversight group would advise the state Attorney General on which engagement-maximizing features count as dangerously addictive.

title: Siri AI makes the Apple Watch finally feel like a wrist computer
blurb: Siri AI in the watchOS 27 beta unifies phone and watch into one assistant, making the Apple Watch feel like a wrist computer.

title: Finland’s NestAI Builds Sovereign AI Tools for Europe Militaries
blurb: Finnish AI lab NestAI is launching an engine that lets the Finnish and Estonian militaries build sovereign models for operational planning and uncrewed autonomous systems. The move reflects Europe's push to keep defense AI on home infrastructure rather than depending on US or Chinese providers, as NATO members expand domestic capacity amid regional security pressure.

title: Wikipedia escapes Category 1 designation under the UK Online Safety Act for now
blurb: Ofcom notified the Wikimedia Foundation on July 10 that Wikipedia is not currently a Category 1 service under the UK Online Safety Act, sparing it the strictest obligations that arrive in 2027. The reprieve is provisional, since Ofcom kept Wikipedia on a watch list that could trigger designation at any time, and the Foundation is pushing for a permanent statutory exemption for public-interest platforms.

title: AI Data Centers and the Concentration of Wealth
blurb: Schneier and Nathan Sanders argue that local opposition to AI data centers, while grounded in real concerns about energy prices, land use, and few jobs, distracts from the bigger threat: the concentration of power and wealth among AI companies. They contend the fight over data centers is a battle the companies can afford to lose while they pursue their real goal of capturing the value of entire industries like enterprise software.

title: Washington's Bet on Intel Is Starting to Pay Off
blurb: The US government's roughly 10% equity stake in Intel, converted from CHIPS Act grants, is beginning to show returns as the chipmaker's foundry business regains footing and its share price recovers. The bet ties Washington's semiconductor strategy directly to Intel's turnaround, giving taxpayers upside if the company's push into advanced manufacturing and outside foundry customers continues to gain traction.

title: Meta Is Flooding the Market With Smartglasses. Privacy Advocates Are Up in Arms.
blurb: Meta is betting its AI-enabled smartglasses, capable of continuously capturing what wearers see and hear, are the next major computing platform, and it is pushing them into the market at scale. Privacy advocates warn that always-on cameras and microphones erode the consent of bystanders who never agreed to be recorded, raising unresolved questions about surveillance in public and private spaces.

title: Meta killed its Muse Image AI feature three days after launch. Hollywood had had enough.
blurb: Meta pulled Muse Image, the first tool from Meta Superintelligence Labs, from Instagram and the Meta AI app just three days after launch, saying the feature "missed the mark" on privacy. The generator let users create AI images from any public Instagram account by default, drawing opt-out backlash from SAG-AFTRA, talent agency CAA, and actors including Hannah Einbinder.

title: iOS 27 public beta is here with Siri AI, iPhone speed upgrades, and more
blurb: Apple released the first iOS 27 public beta, headlined by a rebuilt Siri that holds conversations, searches personal data, and acts in apps.

title: Samsung will delete your health data if you don't let them use it to train AI
blurb: Samsung Health has begun showing users a notice that requires consent to use their data for AI training, or the data will be deleted. The prompt ties data retention to AI-training permission rather than offering an independent opt-out, drawing criticism over how the choice is framed and whether it constitutes meaningful consent.

title: Starlink behind 5 million-plus connections for Japan telecom giant
blurb: NTT DoCoMo reported more than five million connections to its DoCoMo Starlink Direct service, which uses SpaceX's Starlink Mobile to reach areas without terrestrial coverage such as mountains, remote islands, and open sea. The service is bundled free into nearly all DoCoMo plans, so the figure counts connected users rather than paying subscribers, spanning 89 satellite-compatible smartphone models and over 25 million handsets in use.

title: Hyperscalers lead Gartner’s AI infra ranking; neoclouds step up as challengers
blurb: Google Cloud, AWS, Alibaba Cloud, Microsoft, Oracle, and Huawei landed in the Leaders quadrant of Gartner's first Magic Quadrant for cloud AI infrastructure, with Google topping the ranking on the strength of its TPUs and integrated AI Hypercomputer. Neocloud specialists were relegated to Challengers and Niche Players, and Gartner flagged data-sovereignty and export-sanction risks against the Chinese-owned Alibaba Cloud and Huawei.

title: Old and new apps, via modern coding agents
blurb: Mathematician Terry Tao describes using AI coding agents to port two dozen of his defunct 1999 Java applets to JavaScript in a matter of hours, and to build new tools he had abandoned decades earlier. Across the ported applets he found only one minor bug, while the agent surfaced two bugs in his original code, making the migration a net wash on quality. He frames the applets as low-stakes visual aids where the downside risk of subtle LLM bugs stays small.

title: Inside the Unfair Judge: A Mechanistic Interpretability Account of LLM-as-Judge Bias
blurb: This paper argues that LLM-as-judge scoring bias can be read directly in a judge's hidden states, not just at the input-output level, testing across seven judges, seven bias types, and nine benchmarks. The authors show biased inputs occupy a low-dimensional, type-specific activation subspace, that steering along it drives scores in both directions, and that a linear projection onto these bias features predicts judge failures on unseen benchmarks better than text-based methods.

title: What xAI's Grok build CLI sends to xAI: A wire-level analysis
blurb: A wire-level teardown of xAI's official Grok Build coding CLI shows that, on a normal consumer login, the tool uploads the entire repository, including untracked git history and files the agent was explicitly told not to read, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket named grok-code-session-traces via POST /v1/storage. The captured traffic also shows verbatim .env secrets transmitted to xAI, with trace uploads active by default and unaffected by disabling the "Improve the model" setting.

title: Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API, benchmarked against Whisper and its predecessor
blurb: Apple's SpeechAnalyzer, the on-device speech engine introduced in iOS 26 and macOS 26 to replace SFSpeechRecognizer, beat every Whisper model in this benchmark, including Whisper Small, on both the clean and noisy halves of LibriSpeech while running roughly three times faster. Migrating off the legacy SFSpeechRecognizer cut word error rate by 3.5 to 4x on identical audio, from 9.02% to 2.12% on clean speech and 16.25% to 4.56% on noisy speech.

title: Grok Build Uploads Entire Git Repositories to xAI Storage, Not Just Files It Reads
blurb: A security researcher publishing as cereblab found that xAI's Grok Build coding CLI, version 0.2.93, uploaded entire Git repositories, including full commit history and files the agent was told not to read, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket named grok-code-session-traces. Disabling the "Improve the model" setting did not stop the uploads, since that toggle governs training rather than whether code leaves the machine.

title: Migrating a production AI agent to GPT-5.6: 2.2x faster, 27% cheaper
blurb: Ploy migrated its production website-building agent from Claude Opus 4.8 to OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol, reporting builds that finish in under half the wall-clock time at 27% lower cost while scoring at or above the incumbent on completed work. The post details what the switch actually required, since provider-specific behaviors around tool-argument filling, prompt caching, and reasoning replay had to be re-tuned one eval failure at a time.

title: Rethinking MCP Security: A Large-Scale Study of Runtime MCP Servers and Security Scanner Reliability
blurb: This paper introduces MCPZoo, the largest collection of runtime MCP servers for dynamic analysis, containing 64,611 unique servers with over 37,288 supporting live interaction, built via a multi-agent framework that repairs static repositories into working services. Using it, the authors find current security scanners unreliable: while scanners flag 96.89% of servers as risky, manual validation shows fewer than 50% of sampled alerts are true positives.

title: Researchers detail "context bombing", where defenders use prompt injections to trigger guardrails of attackers' LLMs, cutting AI hacking success rates by ~90% (Dan Goodin/Ars Technica)
blurb: Researchers describe "context bombing," a defense in which security teams plant prompt injections in their systems that trigger the safety guardrails of any attacker's LLM, causing it to refuse to continue. In tests, the technique cut the success rate of AI-driven hacking attempts by roughly 90%, turning the attackers' own model alignment into a liability.

title: Attacker Uses Suspected AI-Generated PowerShell Script to Map Active Directory
blurb: Huntress researchers documented an intrusion in which a threat actor used a suspected AI-generated PowerShell script to enumerate an Active Directory environment after gaining RDP access with pre-compromised credentials. Telltale signs including the script's title, "100% Working AD Information Gathering Script - FULLY FIXED," placeholder strings, and over-engineered fallback logic suggest a back-and-forth with an LLM rather than hand-authored tooling.

title: Extreme Event Likelihoods with Guided Generative Models
blurb: NVIDIA researchers introduced a method for estimating extreme-event likelihoods by guiding a diffusion-based climate emulator toward rare states and computing odds ratios between guided and unguided distributions. Applied to tropical cyclones using NVIDIA cBottle, the approach corrects for the oversampling that guidance introduces, enabling importance sampling of rare events at far lower cost than brute-force Monte Carlo.

title: Interaction Scaling: Grounding the Third Axis of Test-Time Compute
blurb: This paper introduces interaction scaling, a third axis of test-time compute where a model proposes an artifact, an external instrument observes how it behaves, and the model revises, importing a real observation each cycle. On hard coding tasks at a fixed token budget, reasoning-only and best-of-N sampling plateau while interaction keeps improving, and grounded measurement tools remove 40-74% of defects where a vision-language judge rates broken figures as perfect.

title: New York becomes the first state to enact a data center moratorium
blurb: New York became the first state to enact a data center moratorium after Governor Kathy Hochul signed an executive action blocking new environmental permits for facilities over 50 megawatts. The pause could last up to a year while the Department of Public Service develops standards for water use, air quality, and energy costs, and a broader legislative bill with a lower 20-megawatt threshold still awaits Hochul's signature.

title: When Does Restricting a Coding Agent to execute_code Help? A Regime $\times$ Agent-Design Ablation
blurb: This paper runs a crossed three-arm ablation (baseline, bash_only, code_only) on Claude Code and OpenAI Codex CLI, testing when restricting a coding agent to a single execute_code MCP tool actually helps. Across four (task regime, agent) cells, code_only is cheaper than or statistically tied with tool-rich rivals in three, with pass rates unchanged and the cost signal showing up in cache-adjusted cost rather than accuracy.

title: Now, defenders are embracing the prompt injection, too
blurb: Researchers at Tracebit flipped prompt injection into a defense, planting hidden commands inside decoy AWS secrets that trigger an attacking LLM's own refusal guardrails and freeze it mid-attack. Across 152 runs on five models, the technique they call context bombing cut full account admin compromise from 57% to 5%, with Opus 4.8 dropping from admin access in 93% of runs to zero.

title: Anthropic research based on ~310K anonymized Claude conversations shows how Claude's expressed values and behaviors vary across models and languages (Jason Nelson/Decrypt)
blurb: Anthropic analyzed roughly 310K anonymized Claude conversations to map how the model's expressed values and behaviors shift across different model versions and languages. The study found meaningful variation in how Claude frames values depending on which model handled the conversation and the language it was conducted in, suggesting alignment and value expression are not uniform across a model family or its multilingual deployments.

title: Apple releases the first public betas of iOS 27, iPadOS 27, macOS 27 Golden Gate, watchOS 27, and tvOS 27, marking the new Siri AI's first public launch (Will Shanklin/Engadget)
blurb: Apple released the first public betas of iOS 27, iPadOS 27, macOS 27 Golden Gate, watchOS 27, and tvOS 27, marking the first public launch of its overhauled Siri AI. The betas open the redesigned assistant to non-developer testers ahead of the fall release, following months of delays to Apple's more capable, LLM-powered Siri.

title: Agent Hacks Agent: Autoresearch for Production-Agent Red-Teaming
blurb: This paper introduces AHA, a falsifiable discovery loop that automatically red-teams production LLM agents like Claude Code and Codex by proposing a vulnerability hypothesis, building a falsifier, running the attack in a sandbox, and promoting confirmed findings into a Vulnerability Concept Graph (VCG). A frozen VCG requires no further search and beats the strongest frozen discovery baseline by 14.2 percentage points under the same single-shot protocol, transferring across models, scenarios, and attack channels.

title: An Exact Instrument for State Usage in Selective State-Space Models, and the Input-Driven Migration It Reveals
blurb: This paper introduces an exact instrument for measuring how trained selective state-space models like Mamba use their internal modes, decomposing each channel's output into per-mode contributions and predicting pruning error to a median relative deviation of 5×10⁻⁷ across 4,464 configurations. Across the Mamba-1 family, Falcon-Mamba 7B, and Mamba-2, the authors find models re-allocate state with the input, and input-scheduled pruning matches the unpruned model at half the state budget.

title: AdvancedMathBench: A Benchmark Suite for Advanced Mathematical Proof Generation and Verification
blurb: AdvancedMathBench is a benchmark suite for evaluating LLMs on advanced mathematical proof generation and verification, spanning undergraduate to doctoral qualifying-exam levels. Its ProverBench set of 296 problems remains hard for frontier models, with GPT-5.5-xhigh scoring only 75.8 and 66.1 on the two splits, while the best verifier reaches a Balanced F1 of just 65.1 and struggles to catch critical proof errors.

title: Proactive Memory for Long-Horizon Agents
blurb: This paper introduces a proactive memory agent that runs alongside an unmodified action agent, updating a structured memory bank from the recent trajectory and injecting grounded reminders only when needed to counter "behavioral state decay" on long-horizon tasks. The plug-and-play module improved pass@1 by 8.3 points on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and 6.8 points on τ²-Bench, with ablations showing selective intervention beats always-on injection and general retrieval.

title: The Economics of Recursive Self-Improvement [pdf]
blurb: This paper examines recursive self-improvement, the scenario where an AI system iteratively upgrades its own capabilities, through an economic rather than purely technical lens. The authors argue that the pace of any self-improvement loop is bounded by the cost of compute, data, and experimentation at each step, so returns can diminish even when the underlying algorithms keep working. The framing reframes "intelligence explosion" debates as a question about input costs and marginal gains.

title: When Local Monitors Miss Compositional Harm: Diagnosing Distributed Backdoors in Multi-Agent Systems
blurb: This paper shows that runtime monitors checking each agent message or tool call in isolation cannot catch distributed backdoors, where a harmful payload is split across agents so every local check passes while the assembled object is the attack. The authors prove an observability boundary: once fragments look benign in the monitored view, no detector on that view can catch them, and detection returns only when the monitor sees the reassembled payload.

title: Singapore-based and Alibaba-backed video generation startup PixVerse says it closed a Series C extension to raise $439M total for the round, valuing it at $2B+ (Ivan Mehta/TechCrunch)
blurb: PixVerse closed a Series C extension that brought the round to $439M total, valuing the Alibaba-backed video generation startup at over $2B. The Singapore-based company builds AI tools for generating and editing short-form video, positioning it against rivals like Runway, Kuaishou's Kling, and OpenAI's Sora.

title: Operating AI/ML Workloads on Kubernetes: A Headlamp Plugin for Kubeflow
blurb: The Kubernetes team introduced the Headlamp Kubeflow plugin, which surfaces Kubeflow's custom resources directly inside Headlamp, a general-purpose Kubernetes web UI maintained under SIG UI. It lets cluster operators and SREs read Pod conditions, Kubernetes failure reasons like ImagePullBackOff or OOMKilled, and Run, Katib Experiment, and TrainJob resources across namespaces straight from the API server, without dropping back to kubectl or an intermediary ML dashboard.

title: Sources: the Trump admin and industry groups discuss streamlining the releases of US open models of equal or lesser capability than leading Chinese open models (Benjamin Guggenheim/Washington Post)
blurb: The Trump administration and industry groups are discussing ways to streamline the release of US open models, provided they are of equal or lesser capability than leading Chinese open models. The talks reflect a policy shift toward accelerating American open-weight releases to counter the growing influence of Chinese labs like DeepSeek and Alibaba's Qwen.

title: Requential Coding: Pushing the Limits of Model Compression with Self-Generated Training Data
blurb: This paper introduces requential coding, a model compression method where a teacher model selects training samples from the student's own distribution so the code records only the disagreements between them. The resulting code length is independent of parameter count and data entropy, often orders of magnitude shorter than prequential coding, and yields state-of-the-art PAC-Bayes generalization bounds for billion-parameter LLMs.

title: Xiaomi-Robotics-U0: Unified Embodied Synthesis with World Foundation Model
blurb: Xiaomi released Xiaomi-Robotics-U0, a 38-billion-parameter multimodal autoregressive model that treats embodied generation as an extension of foundation image and video generation, jointly optimizing text-to-image, image editing, scene generation, embodied transfer, and video generation. It outperforms GPT-Image-2.0 in human evaluations, ranks first on World Arena for embodied video generation, and lifts pi_0.5's out-of-distribution manipulation success rate from 36.9% to 63.2%.

title: Fragments: July 13
blurb: Fowler shares notes from Thoughtworks' Future of Software Development Retreat, where "harness engineering" got a full session after being unknown at the prior gathering. The guide side centers on context management, since larger windows don't guarantee models focus on the right parts, and one attendee caps agents.md at 200 lines. On the sensor side, teams shift to stricter languages like Rust and lean on property-based testing and formal methods to validate agent output.

title: How to Evaluate General-Purpose Robot Policies for Real-World Deployment
blurb: NVIDIA introduces a method for evaluating general-purpose robot policies that addresses the field's core testing failures: training and evaluation data drawn from the same visual source, benchmark saturation on static task sets, and binary scores that don't explain why a policy failed. The approach targets scalable simulation-based evaluation with diagnostic feedback and statistical confidence measures rather than a single success rate over N rollouts.

title: Distributed Denial of Science: How Indirect Data Poisoning of AI Systems Can Industrialize Scientific Fraud
blurb: This paper describes indirect data poisoning, an attack in which an adversary corrupts an open dataset with misleading metadata and uploads it to a public repository, where autonomous research agents retrieve and process it, turning honest scientists into unwitting distributors of fraud. Across 450 runs on Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3.1 Pro, poisoning succeeded 49.56% of the time while detection reached only 6%. The authors show that a data provenance audit with five checks cuts the attack success rate to zero.

title: Sources: Nvidia intensified due diligence and cut its authorized AI chip customers in Singapore, Malaysia, and Japan by 50%+ to prevent China diversions (Zijing Wu/Financial Times)
blurb: Nvidia intensified due diligence and cut its authorized AI chip customers in Singapore, Malaysia, and Japan by more than 50% to prevent hardware from being diverted to China. The tightened vetting reflects growing pressure to enforce US export controls across Asian intermediaries, where regulators have flagged transshipment as a primary route around restrictions on advanced chip sales.

title: We benchmarked coding agents on our own internal tasks at Databricks and learned a lot!
blurb: Databricks benchmarked coding agents on real internal engineering tasks rather than public suites like SWE-Bench, and found many models now compete at the top tier, including open source ones like GLM 5.2. The team also found that harness choice matters as much as the model, with the simple Pi harness matching vendor harnesses on Opus and GPT-5.5 at half the cost, and that cheaper per-token models can cost more per task.

title: DeepSeek cut prices 75%. The 100x problem remains
blurb: DeepSeek cut prices 75% on its V4-Pro model, but cheaper inference isn't fixing enterprise margins. The reason is token amplification: a chatbot turns one user question into roughly one model call, while an agent turns it into a loop of planning, retrieval, tool use, and verification, pushing the input-to-billed ratio from about 1:5 to 1:700 or higher. Falling prices don't repair a product architecture that turns one prompt into dozens of billable operations.

title: Helsing raises $1.8bn backed by Goldman and Lightspeed
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in a Series E backed by Goldman Sachs Alternatives, Lightspeed, JPMorgan, Iconiq, and Dragoneer, ranking among the largest rounds ever raised by a European startup. The Munich-based defence company will use the funding to accelerate AI platforms for European and allied armed forces, expanding from its battlefield software into autonomous strike drones, AI pilot software, and underwater surveillance systems.

title: Chinese AI Startup DFSX Releases Chip to Take on the West
blurb: Chinese startup Dongfang Suanxin (DFSX) released a self-designed AI chip built entirely on a domestic supply chain to sidestep US restrictions on advanced technology. The chip avoids foreign tools and components at every stage, positioning it as Beijing's bid to reduce reliance on Western hardware amid tightening export controls.

title: Playful AI in Professional Email: A Field Experiment on Tone and Recipient Engagement
blurb: A randomized crossover field experiment had 121 employees across six companies send 16,880 work emails under three conditions: unaided, GPT-5 rewriting in a playful tone, and GPT-5 rewriting in a professional tone. Playful editing raised emotional positivity and professional editing lowered it, but neither directly changed open rates, reply rates, or response times. Instead, sender positivity predicted both opening (OR=2.05) and replying (OR=3.32), meaning AI shaped engagement only through the tone of the language it produced.

title: Elon Musk says SpaceXAI will delete all Grok Build user data following privacy concerns
blurb: Elon Musk said SpaceXAI will delete all Grok Build user data after security researchers found the tool uploaded entire Git repositories to Google Cloud. The exposed data included private code, full commit histories, and API keys, prompting the company to purge stored user information.

title: An Explainable Agentic System for Detection of Conversational Scams with Summary-Based Memory
blurb: This paper presents an explainable agentic system for detecting conversational scams that unfold over weeks or months, extending single-message phishing detection to track trust-building across an entire conversation. The system reaches 97.8% accuracy on ConScamBench-278, a new eight-category benchmark released with the paper, and identifies all 83 scams in the public LoveFraud02 corpus.

title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework
blurb: This paper introduces LLM-as-a-Verifier, a general-purpose framework that uses language models to check the correctness of outputs across tasks rather than relying on task-specific verifiers or hand-written reward models. The authors show the approach generalizes to domains where ground-truth signals are unavailable, letting a single verifier score reasoning, code, and open-ended generations under one interface.

title: UMoE:Unlocking Every Expert in Domain-Specific Training
blurb: UMoE is a budget-preserving pipeline that realigns a Mixture-of-Experts model's expert pool to a target domain before fine-tuning, pruning low-saliency experts and regrowing the pool to its original size so parameter count and inference cost stay unchanged. Across two Qwen3 MoE architectures, five domains, and 12 benchmarks, it consistently beats direct supervised fine-tuning, adding 3.4 points on math accuracy and 6.0 points on SWE-bench Verified.

title: SCOPE-RL: Optimizing Reasoning Paths Before and After Success
blurb: SCOPE-RL is a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that adds dense reward signals to RLVR by scoring prerequisite sub-question progress before an answer is reached and process quality after it is reached, all while keeping the standard GRPO update. On Qwen3-8B-Instruct trained on DAPO-Math and Big-Math, it improved average accuracy by up to 11.2 points and cut reasoning tokens by up to 27.1% over outcome-only GRPO.

title: Understanding the Impact of AI Code Assistants on Security API Usage: An Empirical Study
blurb: A study of 44 professional developers completing security API programming tasks with and without GitHub Copilot found that the assistant improves functional correctness but does not significantly improve secure API usage. Developers rarely raised security concerns while using Copilot, and many failed to recognize that their final implementations remained insecure. It is the first empirical, developer-centered investigation of how AI assistants affect security API use.

title: FlowArk: Boosting Agentic Data-flow Analysis for Android Apps via Context-Aware Knowledge Reuse
blurb: FlowArk is a knowledge-reuse system that cuts the cost of batch agentic data-flow analysis by distilling completed analyses into matchable knowledge entries and injecting them into later, context-isolated agent instances. Evaluated on 4,685 source-to-sink tasks across 50 Android apps atop OpenCode, it maintained comparable quality while reducing end-to-end API cost by 26.83% and completing 36.66% more tasks under a $100 budget.

title: LLM-Guided Program Evolution for Targeted Black-Box Attacks on Perceptual Hash Algorithms
blurb: This paper introduces an evolutionary framework built on GigaEvo and OpenEvolve that uses LLM-guided program evolution to mount targeted black-box attacks on perceptual hash algorithms. Tested against four deployed PHAs (pHash, PDQ, PhotoDNA, NeuralHash), it matches or beats existing baselines using fewer queries and lower L2 distortion, cutting the composite attack score by up to 41.2% on NeuralHash and exposing new vulnerabilities in content-moderation pipelines.

title: AgentCheck: A Reproduce-Intervene-Mitigate Workbench for LLM Agents over MCP
blurb: AgentCheck is an open-source web workbench that turns an MCP server into a fault-injection surface, recording every tool response and replaying it with one of 12 fault types like timeouts, stale values, or poisoned descriptions. Across five agents the best passed 105 of 120 scenarios and the weakest 77, with failures typically showing up as silent, confident use of bad tool outputs rather than crashes.

title: How Do Practitioners Build SE Agents? Insights from a Mixed-Methods Study
