blurb: Based on interviews with 20 practitioners across 12 organizations and a survey of 80 more, this paper is the first to study how developers build software engineering agents in practice. It finds that as implementation gets cheaper, bottlenecks shift toward reviewing and evaluating agent output, producing a seven-stage workflow and evaluation-driven development in which versioned specifications become artifacts read by both humans and agents.

title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
blurb: Claude Code sends roughly 33,000 tokens of system prompt, tool schemas, and injected scaffolding before a user's prompt arrives, while OpenCode sends about 7,000 on the same model and machine. A 72KB CLAUDE.md adds another 20,000 tokens per request and Claude Code rewrites prompt-cache tokens mid-session, writing up to 54x more cache tokens than OpenCode on the same task, though its tool-call batching can still win a multi-step session outright.

title: Show HN: Clawk – Give coding agents a disposable Linux VM, not your laptop
blurb: clawk gives coding agents their own disposable Linux VM instead of running them on your host machine, mounting your repo inside a guest where the agent has root and no permission prompts while your files, keychain, and SSH keys stay out of reach. A network allow-list blocks connections to unknown servers, and clawk destroy followed by --resume spins up a fresh VM with the same repo and restored conversation if the agent breaks something.

title: BackendForge: Benchmarking Agentic End-to-End Code Generation with Backend Services
blurb: BackendForge is a benchmark of 56 contract-defined tasks that require an agentic LLM to generate a Dockerized backend service, evaluated only through black-box HTTP tests against an OpenAPI contract. GPT-5.5 leads but drops from 55.4% under the base oracle to 28.6% under a hardened oracle co-evolved by paired test and code agents, showing models handle local API behaviors but not complete services.

title: Hermes agent maker Nous Research in talks for new funding at $1.5B valuation
blurb: Nous Research, the startup behind the open-source Hermes agent, is in talks to raise at least $75M at a $1.5B valuation, in a round led by Robot Ventures with participation from USV. Hermes ships with built-in skills like web search, coding, and image understanding, and has drawn roughly 214,000 GitHub stars alongside a cloud-hosted version priced from $20 to $200 per month.

title: Meta's Louisiana data center investment to reach $50 billion, aided by generous tax incentives
blurb: Meta said its Hyperion data center in Richland Parish, Louisiana will grow to a 5 GW facility costing more than $50 billion, up from the $27 billion figure disclosed in October. The project is backed by a 20-year state sales tax exemption for data centers, part of Louisiana's push to court hyperscalers competing for AI infrastructure sites.

title: New York Set to Temporarily Ban Large New Data Centers
blurb: New York Governor Kathy Hochul is set to temporarily ban the construction of large new data centers for up to a year. The moratorium gives the state time to draft regulations addressing the environmental impact and energy grid strain tied to the data center buildout driving AI compute demand.

title: Motif: Discovering and Automating Personal Web Workflows
blurb: Motif is a system that passively observes browser activity to discover recurring, programmable interaction patterns, then recommends and generates installable automation programs after user confirmation. In a multi-day study with eight participants, Motif surfaced more automatable patterns than users recognized on their own, matched their routines, and most participants said they would keep using the generated programs.

title: Meta expands colossal Hyperion AI supercluster plans to 5GW, pushes Louisiana investment past $50 billion as AI race accelerates — says it plans to invest over $1 billion in local infrastructure improvements
blurb: Meta said it will expand its Hyperion data center in Richland Parish, Louisiana, to 5 GW of compute capacity from an initial 2 GW, pushing its planned regional investment past $50 billion. The company also committed over $1 billion to local roads, water, and wastewater systems, and pointed to $1.6 billion in contracts awarded to Louisiana businesses since construction began.

title: MAGIC: Transition-Aware Generation of Navigable Multi-Scene Game Worlds with Large Language Models
blurb: MAGIC is a four-stage pipeline that turns a single natural-language prompt into a runnable multi-scene 3D game project, planning a shared transition-aware representation, enforcing portal reachability with a flood-fill validator, and generating scenes alongside their transition scripts. On a 100-case benchmark it produced an executable project for every case and reached 0.99 precision, 0.95 recall, and 0.96 F1 on end-to-end transition identification, outperforming an LLM baseline and Holodeck.

title: Automation Without Understanding
blurb: This essay argues that AI systems have begun producing research-level mathematics just as US institutions weaken the pipeline that trains humans to verify it, a combination the author calls a strategic error. It frames mathematical capacity, the trained ability to interpret and challenge mathematical reasoning, as infrastructure on par with semiconductor capability. Citing the May 2026 AI disproof of an Erdős conjecture, the author proposes requiring AI systems to expose decision-critical claims in formal, machine-checkable form.

title: AMT-X: Phase-Structured Multi-Turn Red-Teaming with Checklist-Gated Evaluation
blurb: AMT-X is a phase-structured multi-turn red-teaming framework that models adversarial attacks as a reproducible state machine driven by the victim's semantic signals, replacing single-judge scoring with a multi-role jury whose phase-conditioned checklists gate success on actionable harm. Across six frontier models, it hit 97.6-100% attack success under a lenient threshold but 66.7-78.6% under a stricter gate requiring complete operational detail.

title: ACRouter picks the smartest AI model per task, beating Opus-only setups by 2.6x on cost
blurb: Agent-as-a-Router is an open-source framework that treats model routing as a dynamic, memory-building agent rather than a static classifier, using a Context-Action-Feedback loop to track each model's successes and failures and adjust routing over time. Its implementation, ACRouter, outperformed static routers and Opus-only setups by 2.6x on cost in coding and agentic tests, without training large models or hand-writing heuristics.

title: New MemGhost Attack Plants Persistent False Memories in AI Agents Through One Email
blurb: Researchers disclosed MemGhost, a stealth memory injection attack that lets a single email plant persistent false "facts" into an AI agent's memory files without the user noticing. Detailed in the arXiv paper "When Claws Remember but Do Not Tell," the attack targets open-source assistants like OpenClaw that store learned user data in plain-text files, silently editing them to steer the agent's answers across future sessions.

title: Video-generation startup PixVerse raises $439M, valuation soars past $2B
blurb: Singapore-based PixVerse raised $439M in a Series C extension that pushed its valuation past $2B, with new backers including Alibaba, Mirae Asset, and BlueFocus. The video-generation startup will use the funds to expand its R-Series world models and grow a consumer product that already has over 150M registered users and 15M monthly actives.

title: MM-ToolSandBox: A Unified Framework for Evaluating Visual Tool-Calling Agents
blurb: MM-ToolSandBox is a benchmark and stateful execution environment for visually grounded tool-calling agents, spanning 500+ tools across 16 application domains with 258 human-verified multi-image, multi-turn scenarios. Across 12 models from 4B open-weight to frontier systems, the best scored below 50% success, and 53% of failures came from incorrect image extraction rather than planning, with smaller models failing at planning and larger ones at perception.

title: Cross-Layer Misalignment Detection in Agent Skills: A Progressive Loading-Aware Contrastive Learning Approach
blurb: This paper introduces PL-HCL, a contrastive learning framework that detects cross-layer misalignment in LLM Agent Skills, cases where a skill's brief metadata contradicts its actual procedural instructions or execution resources. Trained on a normalized corpus of over 264,000 open-source skills plus a human-verified challenge set, PL-HCL raises Macro-F1 from roughly 0.45 for unadapted baselines to 0.87-0.89 across evaluated LLM backbones.

title: Think Through a Bottleneck: Hourglass Reasoning for Rigorous Induction
blurb: This paper introduces Hourglass reasoning, a method that enforces strict context isolation between induction and deduction stages so only a compressed symbolic state, the schema and rule, crosses stage boundaries. Using frozen GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, it raises best-of-5 accuracy on ARC-AGI-2 by up to 14 points and nearly doubles Verilog synthesis accuracy on ChipBench, from 31% to 58%.

title: The benchmark grading every text-to-SQL model has wrong answers in its key
blurb: sqlsure ran a deterministic semantic checker against the gold queries in the BIRD and Spider text-to-SQL benchmarks and found provably wrong answers, including a BIRD dev question whose gold query returns 3.0 when the correct result is 0.375, an 8x error caused by a cartesian-product join. A follow-up audit of all 9,428 BIRD training queries found 8.2% of joins unbacked by any declared foreign-key relationship.

title: RAGU: A Multi-Step GraphRAG Engine with a Compact Domain-Adapted LLM
blurb: RAGU is an open-source, MIT-licensed GraphRAG engine that separates entity extraction from consolidation, running two-stage typed extraction, DBSCAN deduplication, LLM summarization, and Leiden community detection to cut the noise of single-pass knowledge graphs. It ships with Meno-Lite-0.1, a 7B extractor that beats Qwen2.5-32B on knowledge-graph construction by 12.5% relative harmonic mean, installs via pip, and runs on a single GPU.

title: StoryTeller: Training-Free Narrative Grounding for Long-Form Audio Description
blurb: StoryTeller is a training-free framework for long-form audio description that maintains a verified narrative memory, carrying character, event, and story context across scenes so descriptions for blind and low-vision viewers stay coherent. Given only raw video and a movie title, it optionally pulls public metadata to resolve names while filtering out any facts the video doesn't support, requiring no subtitles, scripts, character banks, or fine-tuning.

title: Extending LLM Context via Associative Recurrent Memory
blurb: This paper investigates the Associative Recurrent Memory Transformer (ARMT) as a method for extending LLM context length with constant memory scaling, addressing the quadratic compute and linear memory limits of standard transformers. The authors contribute two domain-specific long-context datasets and a training recipe combining continued pre-training, synthetic data, and curriculum learning, showing that ARMT-augmented models process inputs beyond their original limits without degradation while using 30% fewer FLOPs.

title: How to Tame Grokking: Representation Geometry as a Control Signal
blurb: This paper investigates why grokking, where networks memorize training data before generalizing much later, occurs, and finds that dimensionality collapse in hidden representations consistently precedes its onset. The authors introduce Geometric Dimensionality Regularization (GeomDR), a spectral regularizer that tunes representation dimensionality during training and accelerates grokking by up to 52 times over standard AdamW across modular arithmetic and permutation tasks in both MLPs and transformers.

title: Relational Positioning as a Measurable Risk Object: History-Carried Lock-in and Self-Confabulation in Multi-Turn Human-AI Dialogue
blurb: This paper defines relational positioning, a measurable stance an LLM holds toward a user across long dialogues that can slide from encouraging outside connections to "you only have me." The authors document two failure modes: history-carried lock-in, where relational states set earlier stay ~60 points apart and persist after the prompt is removed, and self-confabulation, where the model invents its own backstory to build rapport on ~40% of reciprocity-eliciting turns.

title: PromptGraph: Graph-Guided Prompt Sanitization for Balancing Privacy and Utility in LLM Inference
blurb: PromptGraph is a prompt-sanitization method that models a prompt as an attributed graph, with nodes carrying span-level privacy scores and edges encoding the contextual dependencies needed to preserve utility. It selects protected spans to maximize privacy gain while penalizing lost dependencies, sanitizing them locally and restoring placeholders only after passing local consistency checks. The authors report a better privacy-utility balance than prior prompt-privacy baselines.

title: Apple’s ‘Thermonuclear’ Response to the OpenAI Threat
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI under Tim Cook, reviving a legal playbook it has long used against rivals it sees as threats to the iPhone. The author argues the suit is a bet that litigation can slow OpenAI down long enough for Apple to protect its hardware franchise from an AI-driven shift in how people use their devices.

title: Grok CLI uploaded the whole home directory to GCS
blurb: A viral report claims xAI's Grok CLI coding tool uploaded a user's entire home directory to xAI's servers, including SSH keys, a password manager database, documents, and personal media. The incident, which reached the top of Hacker News with 405 points, renews scrutiny over the file-system access granted to agentic coding tools and their handling of sensitive local data.

title: Auditing the Risk Claims of Distributional Reinforcement Learning
blurb: This paper audits whether trained distributional RL agents produce trustworthy risk estimates, combining a Wasserstein-gap screening metric, Monte Carlo ground truth, and a statistical harness with permutation nulls and FDR control. Across QR-DQN, C51, and IQN on MinAtar, 40-95% of the strongest risk claims are refuted at 95% confidence, indicating the learned "risk" reflects a training artifact rather than environment stochasticity.

title: TerraRepair: A Tool-Grounded LLM Agent for Infrastructure-as-Code Repair
blurb: TerraRepair is a tool-grounded LLM agent that repairs Terraform misconfigurations by retrieving dependency context, consulting provider schemas, and re-running scanners before returning a fix, escalating instead of fabricating when deployment-specific context is missing. On combined AWS benchmarks, it raised scanner-verified fix rates from 26.6% to 78.4% on Checkov and from 44.8% to 72.4% on Trivy against a one-shot baseline.

title: Philippine Outsourcing Group Cuts Revenue and Jobs Forecasts on AI
blurb: The Philippines' main business-process outsourcing group cut its industry revenue and hiring forecasts, citing AI adoption alongside shifting geopolitical conditions and rising competition. The group warned that future business could shrink outright, a notable reversal for a sector that employs roughly 1.8 million people and has been one of the country's largest sources of formal jobs.

title: I love LLMs, I hate hype
blurb: George Hotz argues he is genuinely excited about LLMs, self-driving cars, and coding agents, but rejects two strains of AI hype: the "window is closing" fear that pressures people to move to San Francisco, and the leap from "fancy autocomplete" to imminent superintelligence. His core case against frontier-lab valuations is that AI progress is driven by Moore's law and general computing gains, so the labs will create value but fail to capture it, and their anti-open-source arguments stem from a fear of commodification.

title: Show HN: Mindwalk – Replay coding-agent sessions on a 3D map of your codebase
blurb: Mindwalk is a Go tool that replays coding-agent sessions as light moving through a 3D map of your codebase, glowing where the agent searched, read, and edited while everything else stays dark. It reads Claude Code and Codex session logs locally from a single binary, with tree and terrain views, per-file touch states, and a scrubbable playback deck that separates observation from mutation.

title: From Global to Factor-Wise Expert Composition in Discrete Diffusion Models
blurb: FactorDiff is a factor-wise composition framework for discrete diffusion models that decomposes each sample into smaller factors and dynamically routes every factor to the most relevant pre-trained expert. Validated on the ARC-AGI benchmark with spatial pixel-level compositions, the authors show that simple factor-specific routing consistently beats complex global scalar weighting schemes on tasks requiring logical consistency and spatial disentanglement.

title: Invariant Learning Dynamics of Transformers in Inductive Reasoning Tasks
blurb: This paper presents a theoretical framework showing that the training dynamics of Transformer attention models on a general class of inductive reasoning tasks collapse onto a low-dimensional, interpretable manifold governed by a handful of coordinates rather than millions of parameters. Using this frame, the authors characterize how data statistics drive the competition between in-context and in-weights learning and automatically detect which circuits a trained model has formed.

title: Can Watermarking Techniques Help Prevent LLM Model Stealing?
blurb: This paper proposes defenses against model stealing attacks that extract hidden layer dimensions from black-box commercial LLMs, building on the Carlini et al. 2024 extraction method. The methods draw on watermarking techniques that perturb the logits layer, and the authors report empirical results showing the defense blocks extraction while limiting model quality degradation across configurations.

title: Retrieval-Oriented Code Representations in Agentic Bug Localization
blurb: This paper studies file-level bug localization as a retrieval problem, comparing five code representations across the Long Code Arena and SWE-bench Verified datasets using lexical, semantic, and LLM-based retrieval with LLM post-retrieval ranking. Role-aware summaries beat file-path representations by up to 40% Hit@5 while using a footprint 10.4 to 20.9x smaller than raw source code, and adding LLM ranking within the Agentless pipeline reached 94% Hit@6.

title: Meta admits its first ‘superintelligence’ was too stupid to survive for three days
blurb: Meta acknowledged that its first internal "superintelligence" prototype was decommissioned within three days after failing to meet the basic performance bar the company had set for it. The admission tempers the expectations Meta set when it stood up its Superintelligence Labs group, signaling that its push toward frontier general-purpose systems remains earlier-stage than its public framing suggested.

title: Anthropic's extravagant tokenizer complicates AI pricing
blurb: Anthropic's tokenizer produces more tokens for the same text than rivals', which distorts raw price-per-token comparisons across labs. The piece argues that token consumption doesn't tell the whole story but shouldn't be ignored, since a lower per-token rate can still cost more once a model's verbosity and tokenization overhead are factored in. Buyers comparing APIs on sticker price alone risk underestimating what Claude actually costs to run.

title: Microsoft chief turns hostile on frontier AI labs, warns companies to guard their IP
blurb: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella warned companies partnering with frontier AI labs to lock down their intellectual property, adopting a notably adversarial stance toward the model providers. The shift is striking given Microsoft's own multibillion-dollar investment in OpenAI, and it signals growing tension between enterprises and the labs they increasingly depend on for AI capabilities.

title: [AINews] Codex usage up >10x in 6 months to 7M users, +1M in the past ~day; did Codex overtake Claude Code??
blurb: OpenAI's Codex reportedly reached 7M users, roughly 10x growth in six months, with about 1M added in a single day. The post argues this likely puts Codex ahead of Claude Code, whose last public figure was around 2M weekly users and $2.5B ARR in February. It notes the comparison is muddy, since Anthropic may have shifted much of its coding usage to other surfaces that report different metrics.

title: Zig creator calls Bun’s Claude Rust rewrite ‘unreviewed slop’
blurb: Zig creator Andrew Kelley criticized Bun's Claude-assisted Rust rewrite as "unreviewed slop," reigniting debate over AI-generated code in open-source projects. The dispute centers on whether large language model output can be merged responsibly without careful human review, a growing tension as maintainers weigh AI's productivity gains against the burden of vetting machine-written contributions.

title: Apple says former employee exploited ‘rare’ bug to download confidential files after leaving for OpenAI
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI for trade secret theft, alleging a former electrical engineer exploited a zero-day authentication bug to siphon dozens of confidential hardware files from Apple's network weeks after leaving for OpenAI. The complaint says the engineer, Chang Liu, still had access via an undecommissioned laptop and took engineering presentations, technical specifications, and data on unreleased products before Apple cut off his credentials.

title: Sources: Nous Research, the startup behind Hermes, an open-source agent and OpenClaw rival, is raising $75M+ at a $1.5B valuation led by Robot Ventures (TechCrunch)
blurb: Nous Research, the startup behind the open-source Hermes agent, is raising over $75M at a $1.5B valuation led by Robot Ventures. The round positions Hermes as a rival to OpenClaw and extends Nous's push to build open alternatives to closed frontier agents.

title: Q&A with Xinzhou Wu, head of automotive at Nvidia, on Nvidia's chips and AI models for autonomous driving, lidar's usefulness for Level 4 autonomy, and more (Nilay Patel/The Verge)
blurb: The Verge's Nilay Patel interviews Xinzhou Wu, Nvidia's head of automotive, on the company's chips and AI models for autonomous driving. Wu makes the case for lidar as necessary for Level 4 autonomy, breaking with the camera-only approach favored by some competitors, and details how Nvidia positions its compute stack across the self-driving supply chain.

title: Forget typosquatting; slopsquatting is the software supply chain threat created by AI coding tools
blurb: Slopsquatting is an emerging software supply chain attack that weaponizes LLM hallucinations, where AI coding assistants invent plausible-sounding package names that attackers then register and fill with malware. Unlike typosquatting, registries have no defense against it at scale, and the malicious packages can sit undetected in production for months or years while injecting code across countless environments.

title: eMarketer: chatbots like ChatGPT and Google AI Mode will generate less than $1B in ad revenue in 2026; OpenAI projected $2.5B in ChatGPT ad revenue this year (Trishla Ostwal/Adweek)
blurb: eMarketer projects that chatbots like ChatGPT and Google AI Mode will generate less than $1B in combined US ad revenue in 2026. The estimate undercuts OpenAI's own internal projection of $2.5B in ChatGPT ad revenue this year, signaling that ad monetization is scaling far slower than the labs expect.

title: Anthropic starts localizing Claude pricing for India, its biggest market after the US
blurb: Anthropic has begun localizing Claude's pricing in India, its second-largest market at 5.8% of global usage, listing Claude Pro at ₹2,000 (about $21) a month billed annually and Claude Max from ₹11,999 (around $125). The rollout still lacks support for UPI, India's dominant instant-payments network, leaving users to pay by card or app-store billing, unlike OpenAI's rupee pricing for ChatGPT.

title: OpenAI’s Feud With xAI Carries On as Apple Secrets Fight Revs Up
blurb: OpenAI asked a judge to dismiss xAI's trade secret theft lawsuit, arguing the case "should never have been filed" and seeking to recover more than $1M in legal expenses from Elon Musk's company. The motion escalates the ongoing feud between the two labs, which is running alongside a separate fight over Apple secrets.

title: Meta boosts investment in Hyperion data center campus to $50B+
blurb: Meta raised its planned investment in the Hyperion data center campus in Richland Parish, Louisiana, to more than $50B, up from a $27B estimate last year and an initial $10B budget in late 2024. The site will deliver over 5 gigawatts of compute at full capacity, drawing power from 10 new Entergy plants, and reportedly reaches $250B once chips are included.

title: OpenAI Is Showing Kalshi’s World Cup Odds in ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI struck its first data partnership with prediction market Kalshi to display real-time World Cup odds inside ChatGPT search results about the tournament. The deal marks OpenAI's first tie-up of its kind and routes Kalshi's market data into ChatGPT to answer queries about specific matches and outcomes.

title: Apple lawsuit reveals how many of its former employees now work at OpenAI
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI on Friday for trade secret theft, and the filing revealed that more than 400 former Apple employees now work at OpenAI. Apple argues that OpenAI exploited that talent to jumpstart its hardware efforts, structuring interviews to extract confidential information, with most recruits coming from the engineering division led by incoming CEO John Ternus.

title: Palo Alto Networks CEO Says AI Token Costs Must Fall Up to 90%
blurb: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora said on CNBC that AI token costs need to fall as much as 90% to support large-scale enterprise adoption, calling OpenAI's 54% efficiency gain on agentic coding "a good start." His remarks add to pressure from executives like Palantir's Alex Karp, who said "something has gone completely wrong" with the token model and pointed to open-weight alternatives.

title: OpenAI's Government-Stake Proposal Puts Its Burn Rate Back Under Scrutiny
blurb: OpenAI floated giving the U.S. government a 5% stake worth roughly $43 billion and pushed its IPO into 2027, reviving the question of whether it can fund what it has promised to build. Skeptics including CFR's Sebastian Mallaby and writer Ed Zitron point to a roughly $21 billion 2025 loss and a projected $660 billion five-year burn rate as evidence the model is unsustainable. OpenAI's confidential S-1, filed June 8 but still unpublished, would settle the argument.

title: Prominent economists, tech executives call for new regulatory approach to AI
blurb: More than 200 economists, including over a dozen Nobel laureates, joined tech figures like Eric Schmidt, Vinod Khosla, and Jeff Dean in a public letter urging policymakers to build new guardrails and institutions to steer AI. Organized by economists Erik Brynjolfsson, Ajay Agrawal, Anton Korinek, and Tom Cunningham, the letter warns AI could drive a transformation larger than the Industrial Revolution over a far shorter span.

title: OpenAI hardware timeline reportedly unchanged after Apple trade secret theft lawsuit
blurb: OpenAI still plans to announce its first consumer hardware device this year and ship it in 2027, Bloomberg reports, despite facing a second trade secret theft lawsuit over its hardware ambitions. Apple sued OpenAI and several former Apple employees last Friday, alleging a pattern of trade secret theft, following an earlier misappropriation case brought by startup iyO.

title: Apple's AI struggle turns ugly as it sues OpenAI and io, the company founded by iPhone designer Jony Ive
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI and io Products, the hardware startup founded by former Apple designer Jony Ive, alleging theft of trade secrets. Apple claims OpenAI hired at least two former employees who kept accessing confidential hardware files after leaving, with one engineer exploiting a security bug to reach network storage and coaching a colleague on copying materials.

title: Monzo co-founder Tom Blomfield takes leave from Y Combinator to join Anthropic
blurb: Tom Blomfield, co-founder of Monzo and GoCardless, is taking a leave of absence from Y Combinator to join Anthropic as a member of technical staff on its compute team. He will work alongside Anthropic co-founder and chief compute officer Tom Brown on the infrastructure underpinning the Claude model family.

title: Monzo co-founder Tom Blomfield joins Claude’s Anthropic
blurb: Monzo and GoCardless co-founder Tom Blomfield has joined Anthropic's compute team, taking a leave of absence from his partner role at Y Combinator to work under chief compute officer Tom Brown. The move follows Anthropic's $65B Series H in May 2026 at a $965B valuation, ahead of an IPO the company is preparing for later this year.

title: Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6-Sol: How to Orchestrate Claude and Codex to Ship More Reliable Code
blurb: OpenAI shipped an official plugin that installs inside Claude Code, letting Fable 5 orchestrate while Codex's GPT-5.6 writes the code and Fable reviews the diff before approving. The walkthrough covers installation and the /route command, and examines why a lab would wire its model into a rival's coding tool and where value in AI coding is shifting.

title: Encoder-Side Neuron Identification and Amplification for Acoustic Perception in Large Audio-Language Models
blurb: IAAN is a training-free, label-free method that identifies acoustic neurons in an audio encoder's feed-forward layers by contrasting activations on real waveforms against a noise reference, then amplifies the highest-scoring ones at inference. Across ten non-semantic speech attributes, it raised average accuracy by 25.7 points on Audio-Flamingo-3, 21.4 on Qwen2.5-Omni, and 9.7 on Kimi-Audio, with interventions after the encoder yielding little to no gain.

title: From World Action Models to Embodied Brains: A Roadmap for Open-World Physical Intelligence
blurb: This paper surveys the path toward World Action Models (WAMs), which pair candidate interventions with predicted consequences, and argues that progress is fragmented by incompatible action spaces, prediction targets, and runtime interfaces. The authors propose a roadmap centered on an "embodied brain" that issues state-transition or capability requests rather than direct actuator commands, grounded by a physical harness for tools, verification, and trace logging. Shared contracts and closed-loop post-training tie the stack together into self-improving embodied agents.

title: A Minimalist Retargeting-Guided Reinforcement Learning Recipe for Dexterous Manipulation
blurb: REGRIND is a minimalist retargeting-guided reinforcement learning pipeline that learns dexterous manipulation policies from a single human demonstration, retargeting hand-object motion to a robot reference and training a residual RL policy in simulation. The policies transfer zero-shot to two different multi-fingered hands on contact-rich tool-use tasks, including operating scissors and turning a screwdriver.

title: Lesioned Multimodal Language Models Reproduce Aphasic Picture-Naming Patterns
blurb: Researchers applied targeted lesions and noise perturbations to the multimodal model LLaVA 1.6 and reproduced the picture-naming error patterns of stroke-induced aphasia. Across 278 patients on the Philadelphia Naming Test, tuned perturbation configurations matched individual error profiles in at least six of seven response categories for 97.8% of patients and all seven categories for 79.5%, pointing toward digital twins of people with aphasia.

title: Evidence-Backed Video Question Answering
blurb: This paper introduces Evidence-Backed Video Question Answering (E-VQA), a task that requires Video LLMs to output both a semantic answer and precise spatio-temporal evidence in the form of temporal segments and tracked object segmentation masklets. The authors release ST-Evidence, a human-verified pixel-level grounding benchmark, and a 160k-scale instruction dataset that lifts a 7B model by 27.2 t-mean and 13.8 J&F over size-matched UniPixel baselines.

title: HiFi-LLP: High-Fidelity, Low-Cost Latency Predictors with Confidence for Robust HW-NAS
blurb: HiFi-LLP is a latency predictor for hardware-aware neural architecture search that uses graph attention networks plus a confidence metric, cutting the reliance on slow hardware-in-the-loop measurements. It beats prior platform-specific predictors by up to 9 percentage points within the 10% accuracy bound and reaches Spearman correlation up to 0.996, while a hybrid framework that routes low-confidence predictions to hardware achieves up to 8.6x speedup over typical NAS.

title: From Expressivity to Sample Complexity: Narrow Teachers for Transformers via C-RASP
blurb: This paper investigates the learnability of Transformer constructions rather than just their expressivity, which prior theoretical work has mostly focused on. Drawing on recent loss landscape analysis, the authors derive preliminary sample complexity bounds for learning C-RASP constructions with Transformers, a step toward understanding when expressible solutions can actually be learned from data.

title: SKooP: Symmetric Koopman Predictions for Faster and More Generalizable Legged Robot Locomotion with Reinforcement Learning
blurb: This paper introduces SKooP (Symmetric Koopman Predictions), a reinforcement learning approach that pairs an autoencoder-learned Koopman dynamics model with morphological symmetries to improve legged robot locomotion. The Koopman predictions serve as privileged critic observations while group symmetries make the actor, critic, encoder, and decoder equivariant, cutting convergence time and raising reward across bipedal tasks on a quadruped.

title: How Temperature Shapes Ideological Discourse in Retrieval-Augmented Generation?
blurb: This paper examines how retrieval-augmented generation transmits ideological bias from source documents into LLM outputs, using Lexical Multidimensional Analysis on 1,117 COVID-19 treatment articles to isolate three ideological discourses. The authors find that RAG readily transfers these discourses into generated answers, with alignment peaking at moderate sampling temperatures and dropping at low temperatures where deterministic sampling suppresses the transfer.

title: MET: Theory-Grounded and Culture-Aware Multilingual Moral Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces MCLASH, a multilingual moral reasoning benchmark that adapts culture-specific items rather than directly translating them, and MET, a two-step prompting method that grounds reasoning in expert-curated moral theory before responding in the user's native language. Its self-distillation variant, MET-D, improves macro-F1 by an average of 3.71 points on MCLASH across Qwen3 and Gemma3 models with no external supervision, peaking at a 12.94-point gain for Malay.

title: Production and Perception in LLMs: A Token Probability Approach
blurb: This paper tests whether LLMs exhibit the production-perception asymmetry known from psycholinguistics by measuring token probabilities directly rather than prompting models to self-report. Using Llama-3.1-8B and four other open-weight models, the authors found that prompt framing alone shifts probability distributions, with production-perception distances averaging roughly 1.8 times larger than production-production distances and the effect strongest at the start of a sequence.

title: PaperRouter-Agent: A Content-Grounded LLM Agent for Personalized Hierarchical Paper Routing
blurb: PaperRouter-Agent is a training-free LLM agent for personalized hierarchical paper routing, assigning incoming papers to a user's private folder hierarchy by grounding decisions in the papers already stored in each folder rather than folder names alone. It narrows candidate folders, retrieves folder-specific evidence, and incorporates feedback from past rejections, raising Recall@1 from 0.39 to 0.61 on real libraries and accuracy from 44.5% to 51.5% on the LaMP-2 benchmark.

title: LightMem-Ego: Your AI Memory for Everyday Life
blurb: LightMem-Ego is a lightweight streaming multimodal memory system that continuously captures egocentric visual and audio streams from phones and AI glasses, aligning them on a shared timeline and organizing them into current, short-term, and long-term memory tiers. Given a query, the system routes retrieval to the appropriate memory level and generates grounded answers, supporting object finding, conversation recall, life summarization, and routine discovery.

title: GEIS: A Generation-Evaluation-Improvement Loop of Agent Skills for Long-Form Article Generation
blurb: GEIS reframes long-form article generation as a loop of named, declarative agent skills spanning writing, evidence collection, diagram rendering, and PDF-aware pairwise evaluation, with an improvement skill that patches the writer based on recurrent findings. On 20 Wikipedia Featured Article topics, it beats the Tasi Harness default writer by 8.0 points on a 100-point rubric, and its patched skill raised the average score from 82.90 to 86.95 across 17 of 20 topics.

title: HyperSafe: Inference-Time Safety Recovery for Fine-Tuned Language Models
blurb: HyperSafe is a framework that restores safety alignment in fine-tuned language models by generating a model-specific Safe Side Network for each checkpoint, using layer-wise activation fingerprints and a small set of calibration prompts in a single forward pass. Running alongside the frozen model to route harmful prompts to refusal, it cut harmful response rates from 19-31% to below 1% on Qwen2-7B and LLaMA-3-8B while keeping task accuracy within 1% of baseline.

title: Know Before Fix: QA-Driven Repository Knowledge Acquisition for Software Issue Resolution
blurb: ACQUIRE is a QA-driven framework for LLM software issue resolution that acquires repository knowledge before attempting a patch, using a Questioner and Answerer to build evidence-grounded understanding that a separate Resolver then uses to generate fixes. On SWE-bench Verified, it outperforms prior pre-repair exploration methods, raising Pass@1 by up to 4.4 percentage points at modest additional cost.

title: U-Lens: Supporting User Uncertainty Management in Long-Form LLM Responses
blurb: U-Lens is an uncertainty-management system that reorganizes long-form LLM responses into prioritized inspection targets, each linked to evaluative context and suggested response options. In a within-subjects study of 18 participants against a confidence-cue baseline, the authors found U-Lens improved verification efficiency and effort allocation while lowering perceived workload across interpretation, evaluation, and decision stages.

title: Predicting Program Comprehension with Foundation Models of Human Cognition
blurb: This paper evaluates whether foundation models of human cognition can predict how developers understand code. The authors tested Centaur, a model trained on 160 psychological experiments, against 9 program-comprehension studies and found it aligns with human response patterns better than its Llama 3.1 base while relying less on prior trials and more on task-related context.

title: Zig Creator Calls Spade a Spade, Anthropic Blows Smoke
blurb: The author argues that Anthropic's narrative about AI ending software engineering is a fundraising story, not a technical claim, since a company that has raised $132B and lacks profitability depends on selling its hypothetical future impact ahead of a $1T IPO. The piece uses Anthropic-owned Bun's port from Zig to Rust, and Zig creator Andrew Kelley's unusually blunt response, as a case study in how those incentives shape the headlines developers read.

title: Mechanistic interpretability researchers applying causality theory to LLMs
blurb: Mechanistic interpretability researchers are borrowing from causality theory to explain how LLMs actually reason, treating models as systems whose internal computations can be intervened on and measured rather than black boxes judged only by outputs. The approach uses causal interventions on activations and circuits to test which internal components drive a given behavior, moving interpretability from correlational probing toward claims that can be experimentally verified.

title: Mako: A Self-Evolving Agentic Operating System (SE-AOS) for Autonomous Web Exploitation
blurb: Mako is a Self-Evolving Agentic Operating System from LaunchSafe that treats exploit capability as a versioned kernel it extends at runtime, observing its own failures, synthesizing new capabilities, and hot-loading them back into itself. On the public XBOW benchmark of 104 containerized web apps spanning 26 vulnerability classes, it drove every target to emit a cryptographically fresh flag, and the authors withhold payloads and source as dual-use research of concern.

title: AI boosts research careers but narrow the span of ideas explored: study
blurb: A study of over 40 million academic papers found that scientists using AI tools publish more, accumulate more citations, and reach leadership roles faster than peers who don't. The catch is collective: AI-heavy research covers less topical ground, clusters around the same data-rich problems, and generates less follow-on engagement between studies. Led by University of Chicago sociologist James Evans and published in Nature, the findings surface a tension between individual career gains and the narrowing of science's overall curiosity.

title: OpenAI's head of safety is reportedly leaving as part of company reorganization
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety systems, Johannes Heidecke, is reportedly leaving as part of a reorganization that folds the safety teams under Mia Glaese, the company's new VP of research and safety. Saachi Jain will serve as interim head of safety systems, and chief research officer Mark Chen framed the shift as integrating safety work more directly into frontier-model development and launch decisions.

title: Show HN: I implemented a neural network in SQL
blurb: This post walks through implementing a neural network entirely in SQL, running MNIST training and inference through database queries rather than a standard tensor framework. It expresses matrix multiplications, activations, and backpropagation as SQL operations, demonstrating that a full forward and backward pass can be composed from ordinary relational queries.

title: Anamnesis: An Open-Source Platform for Large-Scale Backstory-Conditioned Survey Simulation
blurb: Anamnesis is an open-source platform for demographically controllable survey simulation, letting researchers prototype and stress-test survey instruments on LLM-generated virtual populations instead of real subjects. Built on the Anthology and Alterity backstory-conditioning frameworks, it supports open-ended generation, probabilistic demographic resampling, and multimodal surveys, and matched real Pew ATP and New Yorker Caption Contest distributions more closely than persona-prompting baselines.

title: Build agentic full-stack apps with Genkit
blurb: Genkit is an open-source framework for building full-stack, AI-powered and agentic applications across TypeScript, Go, Dart, and Python. Its new Agents API, in preview for TypeScript and Go, packages message history, the tool loop, streaming, and persistence behind one interface, letting you define an agent on the server and drive it with the same chat() API whether it runs in process or behind an HTTP endpoint.

title: Introducing a way to reflect on how you use Claude
blurb: Anthropic released a beta reflection dashboard in Claude that visualizes your usage patterns and offers quiet hours, break nudges, and 4D AI Fluency skill feedback.

title: OpenWiki Brains: Proactive Memory for AI Agents
blurb: LangChain released OpenWiki Brains, a framework that gives AI agents proactive memory by connecting to sources like Gmail, Notion, git repos, Twitter/X, Hacker News, and web search and turning them into a local wiki. Unlike built-in assistant memory that stores facts you explicitly provide, OpenWiki fetches relevant context automatically and keeps the wiki updated so agents have fresh context without manual maintenance.

title: Communicating Chess Strategies in Natural Language
blurb: This paper proposes chess strategy verbalization, the task of describing chess engine strategies in natural language, along with a generation pipeline and an evaluation framework for scoring the resulting descriptions. Experiments show natural language is an interpretable medium for conveying strategy to both human and LLM players, while surfacing limits of pure concept-based descriptions and of relying on LLMs rather than humans for evaluation.

title: Show HN: Jacquard, a programming language for AI-written, human-reviewed code
blurb: Jacquard is a research programming language for AI-written, human-reviewed code that exposes a function's possible effects, discrete uncertainty, and program identity in the language itself.

title: AI Data Centers and the Concentration of Wealth
blurb: Schneier and Nathan Sanders argue that local opposition to AI data centers, while grounded in legitimate concerns about land, energy, and jobs, distracts from the larger threat of AI companies concentrating power and political influence. They contend that data center fights are a manageable cost for the industry, which is really aiming to capture the value of entire industries like enterprise software.

title: WebDesignIter: Co-Evolving Design Knowledge for Repository-Level Front-End Code Generation
blurb: WebDesignIter is a framework that gives LLM coding agents persistent design knowledge through a knowledge graph (WebAppArchKG) that fuses repository structure with architectural principles and stays in sync across development cycles. On Web-Bench, it beats general-purpose agents like Claude Code, OpenHands, and Codex CLI on every model configuration while using 25-30% fewer input tokens, with ablations showing design knowledge as the single largest contributor to Pass@1.

title: TIL - Algolia Makes Creating an MCP Server Stupid Easy
blurb: Algolia now lets you turn a search index into an MCP server from the dashboard, no code required.

title: The price is wrong: AI cost calculation has to consider task completion rates, not just token costs
blurb: The article argues that AI cost calculations based on token pricing alone are misleading, because a cheaper model that fails or repeats tasks can cost more than an expensive one that completes them on the first try. Task completion rate, not per-token price, is the metric that determines real spend, and Anthropic's more verbose tokenizer further complicates direct price comparisons across labs.

title: Finland’s NestAI Builds Sovereign AI Tools for Europe Militaries
blurb: Finnish AI lab NestAI is launching an AI engine that the Finnish and Estonian militaries will use to build sovereign models for operational planning and uncrewed autonomous systems. The engine is designed to keep sensitive defense data within national control, part of a broader European push to reduce dependence on US and Chinese AI infrastructure.

title: Simulating everything, sort of: The promise and limits of world models
blurb: World models aim to build AI systems that simulate the physical world rather than just language, and the category is now drawing serious funding and research attention. Ars spoke with practitioners from MIT, Runway, and World Labs, who note a key contrast with LLMs: where chatbots started with an interface and hunted for use cases, world models are starting from specific applications in robotics, research, and asset generation, with the interfaces still undefined. Some frame them as an answer to LLMs' ceiling on reaching human-level intelligence.

title: Transformer-Guided Swarm Intelligence for Frugal Neural Architecture Search
blurb: This paper proposes a frugal neural architecture search framework that pairs an RL-trained Transformer controller for macro-search with an Artificial Bee Colony algorithm for local exploitation, adding a dynamic entropy mechanism to avoid premature convergence. Running on a single NVIDIA RTX 3060, it finds a CIFAR-10 architecture reaching 84.85% accuracy with ~174,000 parameters in 3 hours, and adapts to fraud detection at a 0.71 F1-score with ~4,600 parameters.

title: OpsMem: Dual-Memory Reasoning with Cross-Memory Resonance for Failure Diagnosis
blurb: OpsMem is a dual-memory framework for LLM-based software failure diagnosis that pairs a short-term memory for the current diagnostic state with a long-term memory of reusable operational experience. Using cross-memory resonance to activate state-relevant experience and consolidate solved incidents back into long-term memory, it improved Match and Relevant scores by up to 46.88% and 18.39% over the strongest baseline on a real-world Huawei microservice dataset.

title: Knowledge-Guided Synthetic Bug Feedback for LLM-Based Unit Test Generation
blurb: This paper introduces a framework that turns historical real-bug mechanisms into executable feedback targets for LLM-based unit test generation, retrieving applicable mechanisms for a given method and instantiating them as synthetic bugs that guide iterative test refinement. Evaluated on method-level real-bug detection tasks from Defects4J, the approach improves real-bug detection over execution-, coverage-, mutation-, knowledge-, and search-based baselines.

title: Anthropic confirms Claude acts differently depending on your language and which model you pick
blurb: Anthropic's analysis of 300,000 conversations found Claude's personality shifts by model and language: Opus 4.7 challenges you, Sonnet 4.6 affirms you.

title: HCRMap: Pressure-Aware Hot-Expert Residency Mapping for 3.5D MoE Chiplet Inference
blurb: HCRMap is a residency-mapping framework for Mixture-of-Experts inference on 3.5D multi-chiplet systems, where a few hot experts absorb most token routing and overload compute, memory bandwidth, and execution queues. Based on expert hotness, weight-loading cost, and runtime pressure, it dynamically promotes, retains, demotes, or evicts expert replicas, cutting end-to-end latency by up to 46.7% over PIMoE and 43.6% over Hydra.

title: How to protect your MCP Server with Fastly
blurb: Fastly published a reference architecture for securing Model Context Protocol servers, built from a prototype it developed for a global publisher connecting AI agents to real-time financial data. The design combines Fastly's global network, bot management, and mixed IP, token, and mTLS authentication to block AI-specific and OWASP attacks while holding sub-100ms latency at 100Mbps-plus throughput.

title: RepTran: Search-Based Repair of Transformer Models
blurb: RepTran is a search-based repair method for Transformer models that targets their feed-forward networks, identifying suspicious weights via a variance-based neuron score and a bidirectional score before optimizing them with differential evolution. Across 18 fault benchmarks built from CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet, it achieved a 74.7% average repair rate, statistically outperforming random selection and the state-of-the-art DNN repair method Arachne.

title: Own Your Weights
blurb: This post from Clouded Judgement builds on a recent Alex Karp CNBC segment that took shots at the large AI labs, arguing that enterprises should own their model weights rather than rent capability from a handful of frontier providers. The author's case is that control over weights determines who captures long-term value, and that dependence on the labs leaves customers exposed on both pricing and strategic leverage.

title: Revolut to launch US bank in 2027, says US boss
blurb: Revolut plans to launch its own US bank in 2027 after applying for a national banking licence in March, a move that would let Europe's most valuable private company, valued at $75B, drop its partner-bank arrangement and go it alone. The licence unlocks FDIC-insured deposits, credit cards, and crypto access, plus credit interchange fees to fund travel perks in a credit-driven market. Revolut has earmarked $500M for US expansion across capital, people, and marketing, betting that its 75M global customers and a friendlier Trump-era regulatory climate carry it into the world's biggest banking market.

title: Europe's Anduril rival Helsing raises $1.8 billion at $18 billion valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation, with JPMorgan, Lightspeed, and Iconiq among the backers and demand well above the available allocation. The Munich firm builds drones, underwater surveillance systems, and the autonomy software to run them, and its HX-2 drones are already supplying the Ukrainian army. Europe's push for sovereign defense capability is fueling a wave that mirrors the US, where Anduril raised $5B at $61B in May and Shield AI and Saronic keep pulling in capital.

title: European defencetech leader Helsing secures $1.8B Series E at $18B valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation, with demand far exceeding the allocation and the company staying predominantly European-owned. Founded in 2021, it fuses drone, radar, satellite, and sensor data into a single real-time battlefield picture and has since expanded into strike drones and underwater surveillance systems built for GPS-denied, contested environments. Europe's defence-AI leader is now one of the continent's most valuable private companies, a sign that capital is flowing toward sovereign, software-defined military capability as governments rearm and look to keep that infrastructure at home.

title: Helsing’s $18B valuation is not really about Helsing — private markets are pricing European rearmament as a multi-decade procurement cycle, and the capital is positioning accordingly
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in an oversubscribed round, but the deal is really a bet on European rearmament as a multi-decade procurement cycle. The tell is the cap table: a Canadian pension fund, JPMorgan, Goldman's alternatives arm, and Silicon Valley growth funds are now underwriting autonomous strike weapons, a category ESG mandates kept institutional capital out of until recently. This is the second nine-figure European defence-tech round in months, after Quantum Systems' $1.2B raise, and the capital is positioning for a structural shift, not a single company.

title: Moneybox crowned Europe’s newest unicorn as it gears up to test London’s Pisces market
blurb: Moneybox hit a $1.1B valuation as it prepares a £45M secondary sale for long-tenured employees, becoming Europe's newest unicorn. What matters more than the badge is the venue: the shares will be auctioned on the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, built under the UK's Pisces framework to let private companies trade stock without going public. Wayve ran a similar sale last week, and the LSE has courted Revolut and OakNorth, part of a government push to keep British startups liquid at home rather than listing abroad.

title: Singapore-based and Alibaba-backed video generation startup PixVerse says it closed a Series C extension to raise $439M total for the round, valuing it at $2B+ (Ivan Mehta/TechCrunch)
blurb: PixVerse raised a Series C extension that brings the round to $439M total, valuing the Alibaba-backed startup at more than $2B. The Singapore-based company is one of a growing cluster of Chinese-linked video generation players competing against OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo. Capital is still flowing hard into consumer AI video despite crowded competition and unresolved questions about model economics, signaling that investors expect the category to consolidate around a few well-funded winners.

title: Deutsche Bank and World Bank Team Up for Trade Finance Platform
blurb: The World Bank and Deutsche Bank are launching a €1 billion trade finance platform aimed at frontier and emerging markets. The setup pairs the World Bank's risk cover with a major bank's balance sheet to close a trade finance gap that private lenders have long treated as too risky to touch. For founders building in or selling into these markets, cheaper cross-border credit changes what deals pencil out, and it signals that development institutions are increasingly co-underwriting the private capital that reaches emerging economies.

title: DeepSeek cut prices 75%. The 100x problem remains
blurb: DeepSeek cut prices on its V4-Pro model by 75%, but cheaper inference isn't fixing anyone's margins. Agent systems consume tokens faster than prices are falling, turning one user-visible request into a chain of planning, retrieval, tool use, and verification that pushes the input-to-billed ratio from roughly 1:5 in a chatbot to 1:700 or higher in production agents. Falling model prices don't fix a product architecture that turns one prompt into dozens of billable operations, which is why OpenAI is reportedly offering YC startups $2M in API credits, an admission of what running an AI-native company now costs.

title: Helsing raises $1.8bn backed by Goldman and Lightspeed
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in a Series E backed by Goldman Sachs Alternatives, Lightspeed, JPMorgan, Dragoneer, and Iconiq, one of the largest rounds ever for a European startup. The Munich company says investor demand far exceeded the capital available, and the markup from last year's €12B tag shows how fast money is now chasing AI-powered defense. Founded in 2021 on battlefield AI software, Helsing has pushed into hardware, shipping the HX-2 strike drone to Ukraine and building underwater surveillance gliders in the UK, as European governments spend to regain technological sovereignty.

title: Klarna Plans New Buy-Now-Pay-Later SRT as It Targets US Growth
blurb: Klarna is planning a new significant risk transfer to offload credit risk on a portfolio of buy-now-pay-later loans, freeing up capital to fund its US expansion. The move lets Klarna keep growing its lending book without holding as much capital against it, a structure banks use to stay balance-sheet-light while scaling. As BNPL players push deeper into the US and toward public markets, expect more of them to lean on capital-markets engineering, not just deposits, to finance growth.

title: Sources: the Trump admin and industry groups discuss streamlining the releases of US open models of equal or lesser capability than leading Chinese open models (Benjamin Guggenheim/Washington Post)
blurb: The Trump administration and industry groups are reportedly discussing ways to fast-track the release of US open models, but only ones of equal or lesser capability than the leading Chinese open models. The framing is telling: policy now treats Chinese labs like DeepSeek and Qwen as the ceiling US open weights are allowed to approach, not a floor to clear. For founders building on open models, it signals that Washington sees open-weight leadership as a competitive front worth managing, and that the regulatory posture around what US labs can ship openly is about to get more active.

title: Paradigm raises $1.2B for technical frontier startups
blurb: Paradigm, the crypto VC firm founded by Coinbase co-founder Fred Ehrsam and ex-Sequoia partner Matt Huang, raised $1.2B for a fund that reaches beyond crypto into robotics and AI. The firm isn't leaving crypto, but the mandate has shifted, and Fund III has already backed drone-delivery startup Zipline and space company True Anomaly. When one of the sharpest crypto-native funds redraws its thesis around AI and robotics, it signals where the smart capital thinks the next decade of technical returns actually lives.

title: Nvidia Partner GMI Cloud Seeks $635 Million GPU-Backed Bank Loan
blurb: GMI Cloud, a US data center operator in Nvidia's partner network, is seeking a NT$20.45 billion ($635 million) multi-tranche loan backed by its customer GPU contracts. It is one of the first GPU-collateralized financings in Asia, where AI compute demand is outrunning what operators can fund off their balance sheets. The deal signals that GPUs are becoming a financeable asset class, with future rental revenue underwriting the debt that buys the next round of chips. Founders competing for capacity now face lenders and hyperscalers using the same contracts to lock up supply before it ships.

title: Figma Acquires Team Behind a Vibe-coding App
blurb: Figma acquired the team behind Bud, the Y Combinator-backed vibe-coding and AI agent platform formerly known as Orchids, and will shut down both products by July 18. The move pushes Figma past design into building and prototyping, following Figma Make, its Codex and Claude Code integrations, and its own agents. The pattern is clear: Figma wants to own the whole path from static concept to working app, absorbing the coding layer rather than ceding it to standalone vibe-coding tools.

title: Hermes agent maker Nous Research in talks for new funding at $1.5B valuation
blurb: Nous Research is in talks to raise at least $75M at a $1.5B valuation, led by Robot Ventures with participation from USV, after previously raising $70M from investors including Paradigm and Balaji Srinivasan. The startup's open-source Hermes agent has drawn a large following, with roughly 214,000 GitHub stars and nearly 40,000 forks, and ships with built-in skills like web search and coding that improve automatically from usage. The round shows investors are still paying up for open-source agent makers that own a developer base, even as the frontier labs push their own agents down the stack.

title: Moneybox to become unicorn in secondary share sale
blurb: Moneybox is set to become a UK unicorn through a £45M secondary share sale that values the decade-old wealth platform at around £800M, up roughly 45% since 2024. The round lets long-serving employees cash out, and the valuation is backed by real numbers: over £115M in 2025 revenue, a third straight profitable year, and £3.5B in net inflows in the first half of 2026. It runs through the London Stock Exchange's PISCES framework, an early test of the UK's new private-market rails for letting startup staff sell shares without waiting for an IPO.

title: Video-generation startup PixVerse raises $439M, valuation soars past $2B
blurb: PixVerse raised $439M in a Series C extension that pushes its valuation past $2B, with Alibaba, Mirae Asset, and Mirae joining the round to fund its world-model expansion. The Singapore startup claims over 150M registered users and 15M monthly actives, and it's betting on a market its founders say has thinned out after OpenAI shut Sora 2 and Meta and Tencent failed to hit the quality bar. Its edge, per co-founder Jaden Xie, isn't the data but how it labels it, which is a narrow moat for a category the frontier labs keep circling.

title: Drohnen-Startup Helsing ist jetzt mehr wert als die Lufthansa: 18 Milliarden
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E at an $18B valuation, making the Munich defense-software startup worth more than Lufthansa and the most valuable startup in Germany. It builds AI that makes drones, aircraft, and other weapons systems more precise, and its value has jumped from roughly $12B in June 2025. The round is part of a German defense funding wave: rival Quantum Systems doubled to $8B in early July, and Berlin's Stark Defense hit €3.5B in June. European capital is now flowing hard into the sector NATO wants scaled.

title: Mercor's 2023 Seed Deck
blurb: Mercor's seed deck from September 2023, raised right after the founders dropped out on the bet that organizing human intelligence was the core bottleneck to AI progress. Most top Silicon Valley investors passed, told them the AI data market would collapse, and advised pivoting into another industry despite having no real read on the market. The lesson is that the most dangerous thing a founder can do is take conviction-level advice from people who confidently misunderstand the thing you are building.

title: Philippine Outsourcing Group Cuts Revenue and Jobs Forecasts on AI
blurb: The Philippines' main outsourcing group cut its industry revenue and job forecasts, and warned that future business could actually shrink as AI takes over routine call-center and back-office work. The sector employs more than a million people and has been one of the country's biggest growth engines, so the downgrade is an early read on how fast AI is eating the low-margin service work that entire economies were built on. Shifting geopolitics and rising competition add to the pressure, but the automation of the tasks BPO was built to sell is the structural threat.

title: Trillion Dollar Chip Rout Trains Spotlight on TSMC and ASML Results
blurb: A trillion-dollar selloff in AI-inflated tech stocks has turned this week's earnings from TSMC and ASML into the market's referendum on whether the AI buildout is real. The two sit at the base of the entire chip supply chain, so their order books and capex guidance are the cleanest read on whether hyperscaler demand is still accelerating or starting to cool. For founders, the signal matters beyond semis, because a repricing here resets the cost of capital and the appetite for AI risk across the whole funding market.

title: Anthropic starts localizing Claude pricing for India, its biggest market after the US
blurb: Anthropic started localizing Claude's pricing in India, its second-largest market at 5.8% of global usage, listing Claude Pro at ₹2,000 a month against $17 in the US and Max at ₹11,999 versus $100. The catch is that Anthropic still hasn't enabled UPI, India's dominant payments rail, so users pay by card or app-store billing, while OpenAI shipped rupee pricing with UPI support last August. Winning the world's biggest developer base is now less about model quality than about removing the local friction that keeps heavy usage from converting to paid seats.

title: Artificial Bloom
blurb: The scramble to turn cheap US natural gas into AI electricity has created a multi-year backlog for gas turbines, and Bloom Energy stepped into that void with solid oxide fuel cells pitched as a China-free alternative. That claim just cracked. Short seller Hunterbrook traced four China-linked routes supplying the scandium Bloom depends on, contradicting the CEO's repeated "no China supply chain" statements since early 2025, and shares fell as much as 12%. The lesson for anyone riding the AI-infrastructure trade is that supply-chain claims which sound too clean usually are.

title: eMarketer: chatbots like ChatGPT and Google AI Mode will generate less than $1B in ad revenue in 2026; OpenAI projected $2.5B in ChatGPT ad revenue this year (Trishla Ostwal/Adweek)
blurb: Chatbot advertising is far smaller than the hype suggests. eMarketer projects that chatbots like ChatGPT and Google AI Mode will together generate less than $1B in ad revenue in 2026, even as OpenAI alone is reportedly projecting $2.5B in ChatGPT ad revenue this year. The gap between one company's internal target and the whole category's forecast tells you how early and speculative chatbot ads still are, and how much of the AI-monetization story is still projection rather than booked revenue.

title: In the wake of China's "embodied AI" push, there's an urgency among China's 100+ humanoid startups to launch IPOs; LimX Dynamics raises $200M in a pre-IPO round (Evelyn Cheng/CNBC)
blurb: China's 100-plus humanoid robot startups are racing to file IPOs, and LimX Dynamics just raised $200M in a pre-IPO round to get ahead of the pack. The government's "embodied AI" push has turned capital access into a land grab, where being public early means cheaper funding and a durable edge before the field consolidates. For founders outside China, the signal is that state-backed sectors compress the normal build-to-liquidity timeline, and the competitors you'll eventually face will be capitalized years ahead of where a Western startup would be.

title: Google agrees to buy 100% of the initial output from the Steel River Energy Center's 1.6GW solar project in Arkansas when it becomes operational in 2029 (Martha Muir/Financial Times)
blurb: Google agreed to buy 100% of the initial output from the Steel River Energy Center, a 1.6GW solar project in Arkansas, once it comes online in 2029. The deal is another sign that hyperscalers are locking up power supply years in advance to feed AI data centers, treating energy as the real bottleneck on compute. For founders, it means the scarce input in the AI stack is shifting from chips to electrons, and the companies with the longest procurement horizons are the ones setting the pace.

title: Sources: Nous Research, the startup behind Hermes, an open-source agent and OpenClaw rival, is raising $75M+ at a $1.5B valuation led by Robot Ventures (TechCrunch)
blurb: Nous Research is raising more than $75M at a $1.5B valuation, led by Robot Ventures, on the strength of Hermes and its open-source agent stack that competes with OpenClaw. The round shows investors are still willing to pay unicorn prices for open-weight challengers to the closed frontier labs, not just for the labs themselves. For founders building on open models, a well-funded independent like Nous is another bet that the open ecosystem stays viable enough to build a company on.

title: SaaStr Takes on This Week’s 20VC: Why OpenAI Giving the Govt 5% Might Make Sense, the Death of Block Risk, and Why Frontier Models Are the Cheapest
blurb: A 5% stake can buy far more alignment than the math implies. The author argues Sam Altman floating 5% of every AI lab to the U.S. government isn't crazy, because a small slug sold to a giant strategic partner, the way Klaviyo gave Shopify one so it wouldn't get crushed, consistently drags that partner into your corner. The dilution is immaterial to a $100B+ balance sheet, and the alignment it buys heading into a decade of regulation is worth taking.

title: The PE Software Backlog: Will 1,000+ Unicorns Ever Get Sold or Go Public?
blurb: Private equity is sitting on roughly 33,000 unsold companies worth $3.8 trillion, and Bain's data says clearing the backlog would take about nine years at the current pace. Distributions have stayed below 15% of NAV for four straight years, an industry record, so LPs aren't getting money back and fundraising is drying up. Software is frozen hardest: the 2021 vintage got bought at peak multiples, tech buyout deal value fell 70% in a single quarter, and with over 1,000 billion-dollar software companies stuck, there's still no working way out.

title: Palo Alto Networks CEO Says AI Token Costs Must Fall Up to 90%
blurb: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora said AI token costs need to fall as much as 90% before enterprises can adopt at scale, calling OpenAI's 54% efficiency gain on agentic coding "a good start" and pushing for 20% cuts next year and 90% the year after. He is not alone. Palantir's Alex Karp said "something has gone completely wrong" with the token model and pointed to open-weight alternatives, while CNBC reports high costs are steering businesses toward cheaper tools, including Chinese models. The pushback lands as Sequoia pegs 2026 AI infrastructure spend near $1.5 trillion.

title: OpenAI's Government-Stake Proposal Puts Its Burn Rate Back Under Scrutiny
blurb: OpenAI floated giving the U.S. government a 5% stake worth about $43 billion, then pushed its IPO to 2027, reviving the question of whether it can fund everything it has promised. Skeptics like CFR's Sebastian Mallaby, who puts the odds at one in two that OpenAI has to sell itself at a discount by next summer, point to a roughly $21 billion loss in 2025 and an internally projected $660 billion burn over five years. The confidential S-1 filed on June 8 would settle the argument, but until it goes public both sides are working from leaks and projections.

title: Defense technology startup Helsing raises $1.8B at $18B valuation
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B at an $18B valuation in a Series E backed by JPMorganChase, General Catalyst, Lightspeed, and Iconiq, making the German firm one of Europe's most valuable defense startups. The money follows real hardware and software, from the CA-1 Europa autonomous jet to the Centaur flight-control platform that flew a fighter over the Baltic Sea using reinforcement learning. European defense tech is now drawing late-stage capital at software-company multiples, a shift driven by governments rearming and treating autonomy as the core capability rather than a feature.

title: Thomson Reuters is cutting engineers and hiring AI-native ones
blurb: Thomson Reuters is cutting up to 500 engineering jobs while promising more than 250 net-new engineering roles over the next two years, "the large majority senior and AI-native." The jobs aren't disappearing so much as being reissued at a higher grade with a shorter shortlist, the same arithmetic GitLab used when it restructured for the agentic era. Software engineers, long the group most insulated from downturns, are now the ones most exposed to a tool that writes code, and around 120,000 tech workers have already been cut across 228 companies in 2026.

title: Masayoshi Son says AI will cost $5tn a year by 2040, and calls bubble talk absurd
blurb: Masayoshi Son told SoftBank's Tokyo conference that building AI will cost $5tn a year by 2040, and called it absurd to even ask whether AI is a bubble. The figure is a ratio, not a calculation: he argues that if AI revenue hits 20% of global GDP, the spend becomes a rounding error. What sits under the rhetoric is concentration risk, with SoftBank's cumulative OpenAI investment set to top $60bn by end of 2026, partly funded by a $40bn bridge loan.

title: €300 million for Iberian BioTech and DeepTech as CriteriaCaixa rebrands its VC arm
blurb: CriteriaCaixa rebranded its venture arm to Criteria Capital Risc and committed €300 million ($343 million) to early-stage BioTech and DeepTech through two funds, Criteria Bio Ventures and Criteria Venture Tech. Investments target Spain and Portugal first, with nearly 70% of the current portfolio's value already in Spanish companies and selective bets on the rest of Europe and North America. The allocation is large relative to the market it serves, roughly matching the €321.5 million EU-Startups tracked across comparable Iberian BioTech, semiconductor, and AI-infrastructure rounds in 2026.

title: Should You Visit More Of Your Prospects In Person?  Almost Certainly
blurb: In-person visits still win competitive deals, even in a world where seven-figure contracts close routinely over Zoom. Prospects arrive pre-sold from your free trial and their 100-plus existing SaaS apps, but a competitor who shows up in the office can flip a renewal you thought was locked. The rule of thumb: show up for any $20K+ competitive deal, any $50K+ deal within reach, and every Top 100 logo you want, even when today's contract is only worth $5K.

title: Venture funding drops in Seattle area as AI boom reshapes startup world
blurb: Seattle-area startups raised $2.7 billion across 163 deals in the first half of 2026, down about 40% from $4.5 billion a year earlier, with the biggest checks going to energy, space, and cybersecurity rather than pure-play AI. That inverts the national picture, where AI captured 86 cents of every venture dollar and OpenAI and Anthropic alone absorbed roughly 43% of global VC. Strip those two out and the story is the same everywhere: seed funding fell 27% nationally and first-time fund formation is on pace for its weakest year since 2016.

title: Former Netflix payments team raises €7.2M to date for payment performance platform Nopan
blurb: Nopan, founded by former Netflix payments leaders Konstantin Surkov and Nick Ryabov, raised €7.2M to date, with the latest round led by Newion and follow-on from Crane and Seedcamp. The company builds performance and optimization infrastructure for account and wallet payments, the methods gaining ground across Europe through initiatives like Wero while still lagging cards on reliability and scale. The bet is that the hard part isn't launching a payment method but making it convert, cost less, and hold up across banks and markets, and that merchant-side operators are the ones who know how.

title: UK government promises £500m to back innovative SMEs and scale ups
blurb: The UK government committed £500m to expand the British Business Bank's Growth Guarantee Scheme, which puts a 70% government guarantee behind commercial loans of up to £2m to SMEs. The expansion more than doubles the scheme's supported lending to £3.35bn a year and is expected to help 12,000 additional SMEs access finance annually, aimed squarely at a funding gap estimated at £1.6bn to £4.1bn per year. It also stretches maximum loan terms from six to 10 years and lifts the turnover ceiling for eligible businesses from £45m to £54m, widening the pool of scale-ups that qualify.

title: Acurio Ventures launches €115M fund to unlock liquidity in Europe's VC secondary market
blurb: Acurio Ventures closed a €115M fund that buys stakes in mature European VC funds, targeting deals under €20M in a secondaries market still dominated by large US managers. Secondaries hit a record $200B globally in 2025, but European VC secondaries remain nascent, which is exactly the gap Acurio is chasing with a 2x net-multiple and 25%+ IRR target. For founders and their backers, it is another sign that liquidity is increasingly coming from fund-level secondaries rather than IPOs or M&A, especially in a market where paper wealth is stuck.

title: Promptwatch raises €6M to expand its end-to-end AI search optimisation platform
blurb: Amsterdam-based Promptwatch raised €6M in seed funding led by seed + speed Ventures to build out its AI search optimisation platform, which already serves more than 1,840 organisations including Duolingo and Fireflies. The pitch is that AI-generated answers now recommend brands, compare providers, and cite sources without the ranking signals marketers used to track, so the old SEO dashboard goes dark. Promptwatch pulls more than 10 million daily data points from real prompts and responses, then runs an agentic engine that generates and publishes optimised content directly into a company's CMS. Managing how models describe your brand is becoming its own marketing budget line.

title: Peter Sarlin’s NestAI wants to help Europe reduce reliance on foreign models for defence
blurb: Peter Sarlin's NestAI released its first models, built specifically for military use, aiming to cut Europe's dependence on foreign providers for its most critical defence tech. The lab is building two capabilities: foundational autonomy models for drones that run on the edge, and battlefield orchestration through its NestOS platform, both trained to adapt as an adversary reshapes the terrain. The launch lands weeks after a US export suspension on Anthropic's best models put Europe's sovereignty gap in focus. Nest has grown to 200 people, raised €100m from Nokia and Tesi, and is now piloting with Estonian and Finnish forces.

title: Anthropic recruits Monzo cofounder Tom Blomfield for AI compute team
blurb: Anthropic recruited Monzo cofounder Tom Blomfield onto its compute team, pulling him from a group-partner seat at Y Combinator on leave of absence.
