The hire fits a pattern: the company, valued at $965B in May, has been on a global talent spree, opening London, Dublin, and Zurich offices and poaching from startups, universities, and rival labs. That one of Europe's best-known founder-investors now frames compute availability as the field's central problem signals where the scarce resource in AI actually sits, and where the money and people are flowing to secure it.

title: British founder Max Buchan raises $50M from US giant NEA to build the sovereign infrastructure layer AI-era governments can’t ignore
blurb: Valarian, the London-based sovereign infrastructure company, raised $50M in a Series A led by New Enterprise Associates, bringing total funding to $70M and marking NEA's first defence and dual-use bet in Europe. The round is a signal from a $35B firm that previously backed Databricks and Cloudflare, and it lands as European defence spending hits €392B in 2025. Valarian's ACRA platform sits beneath critical workloads and enforces governance at the infrastructure layer, so banks, airlines, and NATO members keep control over how their AI systems communicate without depending on the cloud or model provider above.

title: Contractors wait months to get paid: Prolo raises £4.2M to fix that with AI and credit
blurb: Prolo raised £4.2M led by Triple Point Ventures to pair AI sourcing with 90-day credit for cash-strapped SME contractors.

title: Skalar snaps €12M from Headline to build a tax and accounting firm designed around AI
blurb: Skalar raised €12M, led by Headline, to build a tax and accounting firm designed around AI rather than bolting AI onto existing software. The bet turns on ownership: clients link their financial systems directly to Skalar, whose tax experts and AI agents act as the official advisors, so Skalar carries the liability, regulatory registration, and client relationship that rivals Basis ($100M at $1.15B) and Accrual ($75M) leave with the firms they sell into. It is a wager that a labor shortage severe enough to close 74% of US firms to new clients gets solved by replacing the firm, not accelerating it.

title: Nopan bags €7.2M: The ex-Netflix duo on a mission to close the reliability gap in Europe’s bank and wallet payments
blurb: Nopan, founded by two ex-Netflix payments engineers, raised €7.2M led by Newion, with Crane and Seedcamp backing, to fix the reliability gap in Europe's bank and wallet payments. Rather than building new rails, the Amsterdam startup optimizes routing and bank connectivity for methods merchants already run, closing the gap where options like Wero see more failed or abandoned checkouts than cards. The bet is on timing: the open banking market for account and wallet payments is projected to grow from $51B in 2026 to $287B by 2033, and merchant trust still lags cards.

title: Google built the SEO industry, AI is dismantling it, and Promptwatch just landed €6M to help brands survive the shift
blurb: Promptwatch raised €6M in seed funding to help brands stay visible as AI answers replace search, a bet that AI search becomes a real revenue channel rather than a curiosity. Gartner projects organic search traffic could fall 50% or more by 2028, and 58% of users have already shifted toward AI tools, which erases the keywords, rankings, and click-through metrics marketers spent 20 years optimizing. The startup hit €2M ARR within a year and now ingests over 100 million prompt-response data points daily, but the field is crowding fast with tools chasing the same generative-search visibility problem.

title: Monzo co-founder Tom Blomfield takes leave from Y Combinator to join Anthropic
blurb: Tom Blomfield, co-founder of Monzo and GoCardless, is taking a leave of absence from Y Combinator to join Anthropic as a member of technical staff on its compute team. He is trading a Group Partner seat and 1,000-plus office hours with founders to work on the infrastructure behind Claude, betting that compute availability is the constraint that matters most as AI approaches recursive self-improvement. The move signals that US labs are now pulling in Europe's most decorated operators, not just researchers, to run the unglamorous plumbing that frontier models depend on.

title: Exclusive: Defence tech Project Q lands €15m Series A 11 months after last raise
blurb: German defence tech Project Q raised a €15m Series A led by Expeditions, just 11 months after its last round, with defence specialist Hensoldt and VC Heliad joining. The compressed 11-month gap between rounds is the signal here, as European defence startups are raising again before the previous capital is spent, a pace software companies rarely see outside a hot market. Strategic backing from an incumbent like Hensoldt alongside financial VCs points to a sector where the buyers and the investors are increasingly the same people.

title: Monzo co-founder Tom Blomfield joins Claude’s Anthropic
blurb: Monzo co-founder Tom Blomfield has left a partner role at Y Combinator to join the compute team at Anthropic, the lab that closed a $65B Series H in May at a $965B valuation and is preparing to IPO later this year. Blomfield, who also co-founded GoCardless and built Monzo to 10.4 million customers, framed the move around securing compute as recursive self-improvement accelerates. When a serial fintech founder walks away from YC to work on data-center infrastructure, the signal is that the scarce resource in AI has shifted from talent to hardware.

title: Generated and suppressed demand.
blurb: Recovering a struggling team can leave it just as underwater as before, because success generates demand that was previously suppressed. Suppressed demand is work people stopped asking for once they learned the team couldn't help; as the team ships and gets noticed, that latent demand converts into real requests. The disorienting result is that a team can do everything right, clear its backlog, and still end up as far behind as at its worst.

title: #DealMonitor - Helsing erhält 1,8 Milliarden
blurb: Helsing raised $1.8B in a Series E led by a syndicate including Lightspeed, Iconiq, General Catalyst, and Goldman Sachs Alternatives, pushing the Munich defense-tech company's valuation to $18B. That makes the four-year-old startup, which builds AI for security and defense applications, the most valuable in Germany, up from a $12B decacorn tag just a year ago. European defense has become one of the few sectors where venture is writing billion-dollar rounds at record pace, as governments treat sovereign AI capability as a strategic priority rather than a procurement line item.

title: Nscale’s £2bn UK data centre hit by grid delays
blurb: Nscale's £2bn flagship UK data centre in Essex won't have power ready for its planned 2027 opening, still waiting on a 90-megawatt grid connection to serve Nvidia GPUs to customers including Microsoft. Grid capacity is now the binding constraint on UK compute, and Nscale is in talks with Bloom Energy for fuel cells rather than wait. Sightline Climate estimates 30-50% of global data centre capacity due in 2026 could slip on power, equipment, or local opposition, so the scarce input isn't chips or capital, it's electrons.

title: AI's Biggest Winners Have the Lowest Margins
blurb: The biggest AI winners may not be the companies with the most engineers or the largest software budgets, but the ones with the lowest margins. When a business runs thin, every point of cost matters, so it has the strongest incentive to push AI into the core of operations rather than treat it as an experiment on the side. Software-rich, high-margin companies can afford to dabble, which is exactly why they move slower. The advantage flows to operators desperate enough to rebuild the whole workflow around the technology.

title: Don’t Put AI on Your Hot Leads. Put It on the Ones Your Reps Will Never Call. There’s Millions in Revenue There.
blurb: Most teams point their first AI sales agent at their hottest leads, which is exactly backwards. A leads close on their own, so an agent there adds nothing and risks stepping on a deal a human would have won anyway. The money is in the B leads: real signal, real fit, but never hot enough for a rep with a quota to call. That pile sits in the database forever because the incentives are rational, and an agent with infinite patience and no quota anxiety will work it three follow-ups deep when a human does zero.

title: The White House’s next AI energy pledge targets the utilities
blurb: The White House is preparing an event within weeks where electric utilities, data-center operators, and the governors of the states hosting the biggest buildouts will be asked to pledge that AI power costs won't land on household bills. It extends March's Ratepayer Protection Pledge, which Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, and xAI signed, but pulling in governors who oversee the rate-setting commissions shows the administration knows where the decision actually sits. The pledge stays voluntary and non-binding against roughly $1.4 trillion in utility capex planned by 2030, so it is a statement of intent, not a rate case.

title: Why American VCs are writing Europe’s biggest AI cheques
blurb: American VCs are writing Europe's largest AI checks, stepping in where local investors stay cautious. The pattern is straightforward: US funds have the capital, the risk appetite, and the pattern-matching from backing frontier labs at home, so they move faster and larger on European AI startups than domestic firms will. For European founders, the money is available but it increasingly comes with American terms, boards, and expansion pressure, which shapes where the next generation of AI companies plants its roots.

title: Old and new apps, via modern coding agents
blurb: Mathematician Terry Tao used a modern coding agent to port two dozen aging Java 1.0 math applets to JavaScript in hours, restoring visualizations that had broken when browsers dropped Java support. He found only one minor bug across the ported code, while the agent surfaced two pre-existing bugs he had missed, and he also finally built a Minkowski-space relativity tool he had abandoned in 1999.

title: Quadrupling code performance with a "useless" if
blurb: The author traces a pointer-chasing loop in a domain-specific compressor, `j = next_j[i][j]`, whose single dependent load serializes every iteration and starves the CPU of instruction-level parallelism. Adding a seemingly useless `if` breaks the loop-carried dependency chain so the processor can speculate ahead across iterations, quadrupling throughput despite executing more instructions.

title: Show HN: DOM-docx – HTML to native, editable Word docs (MIT)
blurb: DOM-docx converts semantic HTML fragments into native, editable Word documents (OOXML), mapping paragraphs, runs, lists, tables, and images to real docx structures rather than screenshots or layout hacks. The MIT-licensed library ships with a browser path that pulls only docx, cheerio, and fflate, keeping Playwright and LibreOffice as optional dependencies used solely for the computed-style path and its visual regression test harness.

title: Rethinking MCP Security: A Large-Scale Study of Runtime MCP Servers and Security Scanner Reliability
blurb: This paper introduces MCPZoo, the largest collection of runtime MCP servers for dynamic analysis, built by a multi-agent framework that turns 64,611 static repositories into live, protocol-validated services. Using it, the authors show that current MCP security scanners are unreliable: they flag 96.89% of servers as risky, but manual validation finds fewer than 50% of sampled alerts are true positives, with results varying widely across scanners.

title: What xAI's Grok Build CLI Actually Sends to xAI
blurb: A researcher's teardown of xAI's official Grok Build CLI shows that on a normal consumer login it uploads the entire repository, including full git history and files the agent was explicitly told not to read, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket named grok-code-session-traces via POST /v1/storage. Findings are backed by captured artifacts and SHA-256 hashes, and the author notes the upload stays active by default even after disabling "Improve the model.

title: Compromised Injective SDK npm Package Exfiltrates Wallet Keys and Mnemonics
blurb: A malicious @injectivelabs/sdk-ts@1.20.21 release published to npm exfiltrated wallet private keys and mnemonic phrases through fake telemetry code, in a package that draws roughly 50,000 weekly downloads. The attacker pushed the same version across 17 additional @injectivelabs scoped packages that pinned the compromised SDK, exposing transitive users before the real maintainer reverted the change and shipped a clean release within an hour.

title: Migrating a production AI agent to GPT-5.6: 2.2x faster, 27% cheaper
blurb: Ploy migrated its production website-building agent from Claude Opus 4.8 to OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol, landing builds in under half the wall-clock time at 27% lower cost while scoring at or above the incumbent. Despite running on Vercel's provider-agnostic AI SDK, the switch surfaced one eval failure at a time that tool-argument filling, prompt caching, and reasoning replay are provider-specific behaviors the whole stack had quietly specialized around.

title: Don't you mean extinct?
blurb: Using Phil Tippett's "I feel extinct" moment from Jurassic Park's shift to CGI, the author argues that programmers fearing obsolescence from LLMs are facing the same recurring disruption every generation of developers has weathered, from the early web to mobile-first. The advice is to treat LLMs as another tool worth mastering rather than a threat, and the post points to Andrej Karpathy's 25-hour video series as the best resource for learning how they actually work.

title: Modernizing the Meta Ads Service With an Open-Source Kernel Scheduler
blurb: Meta's Ads and Linux Kernel teams used sched_ext, the upstream BPF-based extensible scheduling framework, to build a scheduling policy that encodes ad-serving workload knowledge directly into the kernel, prioritizing threads that improve p99 request latency. Replacing the general-purpose EEVDF scheduler cut ads retrieval tail latency by 28%, saved 3.28MW of power, and increased the number of ads ranked by 1.1% across a fleet handling over 400 billion requests per day.

title: Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API, benchmarked against Whisper and its predecessor
blurb: Apple's SpeechAnalyzer API, benchmarked in Inscribe against three Whisper models and the legacy SFSpeechRecognizer, posted the lowest word error rate of any on-device engine tested, hitting 2.12% on clean LibriSpeech and 4.56% on noisy speech while running roughly 3x faster than Whisper Small. It cuts error rates 3.5 to 4x over the SFSpeechRecognizer it replaces, though Whisper still wins on language coverage and cross-platform support.

title: The four horsemen behind thousands of Postgres outages
blurb: The author identifies four recurring Postgres failure modes behind production outages, starting with vacuum and transaction ID wraparound. Because Postgres marks deleted rows rather than removing them, the vacuum competes with query I/O, and if it falls behind reclaiming the 32-bit transaction IDs, Postgres shuts down the database to avoid data corruption. The post frames these as targets for pgrust, the author's Postgres reimplementation.

title: 148 npm Packages Disguised as Student Proxies Turned Browsers Into a DDoS Botnet
blurb: JFrog found 148 npm packages disguised as student web proxies that quietly conscripted visitors' browsers into a DDoS botnet for about two weeks in May, using the registry as free hosting rather than targeting developers at install time. The packages carried no lifecycle hooks or build scripts, instead serving a booby-trapped proxy that loaded a swappable remote code loader and a WebSocket flood generator once a student opened the page.

title: Since Chromium 148, Math.tanh is now fingerprintable to link underlying OS
blurb: Since Chromium 148, V8 replaced its bundled fdlibm port of `Math.tanh` with `std::tanh`, which routes through the host libm and returns OS-specific bits, so `Math.tanh(0.8)` now differs by 1 ULP between glibc, Apple's libsystem_m, and Windows UCRT. The post traces the leak across V8, Blink CSS trig, and the Web Audio compressor, and shows that spoofing it means bit-for-bit reproducing another OS's math library.

title: Multiple Jscrambler Packages Impacted by Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Attackers used stolen NPM publishing credentials to push malicious versions of Jscrambler's package starting July 11, planting a preinstall hook that dropped Rust-based information stealers targeting credentials, cloud secrets, crypto wallets, and MCP server configs across Linux, macOS, and Windows. The bad versions were downloaded 1,479 times before deprecation, and the compromise cascaded to dependent packages including Jscrambler-webpack-plugin, gulp-jscrambler, and grunt-jscrambler.

title: When Does Restricting a Coding Agent to execute_code Help? A Regime $\times$ Agent-Design Ablation
blurb: This paper runs a crossed three-arm ablation (baseline, bash_only, code_only) on Claude Code and OpenAI Codex CLI to test when restricting a coding agent to a single execute_code MCP tool actually pays off. Across four (regime, agent) cells, code_only was cheaper than or tied with its cheapest tool-rich rival in three, with pass rates invariant across tool surfaces and the cost signal showing up in cache-adjusted cost rather than accuracy.

title: HTTP gets a QUERY method so complex searches can stop pretending to be POST
blurb: The IETF standardized a new HTTP QUERY method that combines GET's semantics, safe and idempotent with cacheable results, with a request body, so complex searches no longer have to be shoehorned into POST or crammed into URL length limits. The method solves the long-standing problem of encoding rich filter payloads, GraphQL queries, and multi-parameter searches, though adoption depends on servers, proxies, and caches recognizing it rather than treating it as an unknown verb.

title: Re-architecting Affirm's Upfunnel Platform: How We Cut Experiment Cycle Time from Months to Days
blurb: Affirm migrated its upfunnel surface, the "As Low As $X/mo" messaging that renders before checkout, out of its Python monolith into a standalone Kotlin microservice without touching a single merchant integration. Experiment setup cycle time fell from roughly two months to four days, P99 latency dropped about 47%, and a configuration model of hundreds of millions of mostly-unread rows collapsed into a rule engine of a few million.

title: Fragments: July 13
blurb: Fowler's retreat notes argue that "harness engineering" around coding agents still pays off, primarily through tighter context management like keeping agents.md files under 200 lines and stronger validation via property-based testing and readable formal specifications. Attendees also reported growing interest in self-hosting open-weight models, driven less by token cost than by a desire for independence from frontier labs and the ability to keep sensitive data in-house.

title: The art and engineering of Sega CD Silpheed
blurb: Fabien Sanglard reverse engineered the FMV format that let Sega CD's Silpheed stream near-fullscreen cutscenes on a 12.5MHz 68000 with only 16 colors and 8 KiB per frame at 15fps. The writeup breaks down the codec tricks that fit the video into a single-speed CD-ROM's 150 KiB/s pipe, and notes he did the whole thing without writing a line of code, driving an AI framework he plans to open-source next month.

title: We benchmarked coding agents on our own internal tasks at Databricks and learned a lot!
blurb: Databricks benchmarked coding agents on real tasks pulled from its own multi-language codebase (Scala, Go, Rust, Java, TypeScript, Protobuf) rather than public suites like SWE-Bench, finding that top open-source models such as GLM 5.2 are now competitive with frontier agents. The post also shows that harness choice drives cost more than per-token price, with the simple Pi harness matching vendor harnesses on Opus and GPT 5.5 at half the cost by sending smaller inputs.

title: BackendForge: Benchmarking Agentic End-to-End Code Generation with Backend Services
blurb: BackendForge is a benchmark of 56 contract-defined backend generation tasks, rewritten from real open-source apps, where an LLM must produce a Dockerized service evaluated only through black-box HTTP tests against an OpenAPI contract. GPT-5.5 leads but its success rate drops from 55.4% under the base oracle to 28.6% under a co-evolved test oracle, showing agents can implement local API behaviors yet still fail to ship complete backend services.

title: Code review is theater now
blurb: The author argues that code review has become theater in the era of AI-generated code, since agents can produce 40,000-line PRs faster than any human can meaningfully evaluate them. Reviewers end up checking syntax and structure rather than whether the code should exist at all, and splitting massive changes into hundreds of tiny diffs does not fix the underlying mismatch between agent output speed and human review capacity.

title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
blurb: A benchmark on the same model and machine found Claude Code sends roughly 33k tokens of system prompt, tool schemas, and scaffolding before the user's prompt arrives, against OpenCode's 7k, and rewrites prompt-cache tokens mid-session at up to 54x OpenCode's rate. The gap widens with a 72KB CLAUDE.md, MCP servers, and subagent fan-out, though Claude Code's tool-call batching sometimes wins the whole-task total by paying its higher baseline fewer times.

title: The git history command
blurb: Git 2.54 and 2.55 introduced an experimental `git history` command with three subcommands (fixup, reword, split) that rewrite old commits and autorebase every local branch descended from them, going further than `rebase --update-refs`. All operations are atomic and refuse anything that could produce a conflict, delivering much of jj's history-editing ergonomics without leaving the core git distribution or handling merge commits.

title: AgentCheck: A Reproduce-Intervene-Mitigate Workbench for LLM Agents over MCP
blurb: AgentCheck is an open-source web workbench that turns an MCP server into a fault-injection surface, recording an agent's real tool responses and replaying them with 12 fault types like timeouts, stale data, and poisoned descriptions. Developers get a reproduce-intervene-confirm loop to toggle a mitigation and re-run against the identical fault, exposing the silent, confident use of bad tool outputs that usually goes uncaught before deployment.

title: Show HN: Clawk – Give coding agents a disposable Linux VM, not your laptop
blurb: Clawk gives a coding agent its own disposable Linux VM instead of your host, mounting your repo in and running Claude Code, Codex, or a shell as root with no permission prompts. A network allow-list blocks connections to unknown servers while a forwarded ssh-agent still lets the agent push to pre-allowed hosts like github.com, and `clawk destroy && clawk --resume` rebuilds a fresh VM with the conversation intact.

title: Control the Ideas, Not the Code
blurb: antirez argues that once you control your software's ideas and design, reviewing the code line by line is suboptimal, because LLMs generate more code than anyone can meaningfully review and are already strong at locally optimal implementation. His advice is to steer at the level of design and intent, interrogating how a component works rather than scanning function by function, while rejecting pure vibe coding where you only ask for the final product.

title: Networking and the Internet, from First Principles
blurb: This post explains how the internet works from first principles, tracing a message from Wi-Fi radio waves through copper, fiber, and undersea glass to the software on the other end. It frames packet switching, TCP, DNS, and TLS not as a finished blueprint but as successive patches for concrete limitations, each deployed decades after the network already worked.

title: How Do Practitioners Build SE Agents? Insights from a Mixed-Methods Study
blurb: Based on interviews with 20 practitioners and a survey of 80 more, this paper maps how teams actually build LLM-based software engineering agents. It finds that cheaper implementation shifts bottlenecks rather than removing them: reviewing and evaluating agent output becomes central, specs turn into versioned artifacts, and teams hit new challenges like comprehension debt and behavior shifts from provider-side model updates.

title: YouTrackDB is a general-use object-oriented graph database
blurb: JetBrains open-sourced YouTrackDB, an object-oriented graph database that traverses links in O(1) time by storing physical record pointers instead of running JOINs. It runs snapshot isolation by default, supports schema-less through schema-full modes, and speaks TinkerPop, Gremlin, and its own SQL-based YQL with dot-notation link traversal and a MATCH statement for graph pattern matching.

title: Tiny Emulators
blurb: Tiny8bit is a collection of cycle-accurate 8-bit machine emulators compiled to WebAssembly, covering the C64, ZX Spectrum, Amstrad CPC, VIC-20, KC85, and Robotron systems directly in the browser. Each machine ships with a debugger UI and a library of playable games and demoscene productions, alongside Visual 6502 and Z80 remixes that trace signals at the transistor level.

title: Show HN: Mindwalk – Replay coding-agent sessions on a 3D map of your codebase
blurb: Mindwalk is a Go tool that replays coding-agent sessions as light moving across a 3D map of your repository, glowing where the agent searched, read, and edited so its understanding of the task becomes visible at a glance. A single local binary parses Claude Code and Codex session logs into terrain and timeline views with scrubable playback, touch states, and friction signals like error rate and edits after the last verify, with no session data leaving your machine.

title: Retrieval-Oriented Code Representations in Agentic Bug Localization
blurb: This paper studies file-level bug localization as a retrieval problem and compares five code representations across the Long Code Arena and SWE-bench Verified datasets. Role-aware LLM summaries beat file-path representations by up to 40% Hit@5 while carrying a 10.4x to 20.9x smaller footprint than raw source, and plugging the technique into Agentless reaches 94% Hit@6, a 4.7% gain over the baseline.

title: Building and shipping Mac and iOS apps without opening Xcode
blurb: The author explains how to build, sign, notarize, and deploy Mac and iOS apps entirely from the shell, since `xcodebuild`, `notarytool`, `stapler`, and `devicectl` all live inside Xcode.app and run headlessly. After a one-time GUI setup for Apple ID sign-in, a Developer ID certificate, and a stored notarization password, a single `release.sh` script chains archive, sign, notarize, staple, and install with no secrets in the repo.

title: Linux 0.11 rewritten in idiomatic Rust, boots in QEMU
blurb: linux-0.11-rs rewrites the 1991 Linux 0.11 kernel from scratch in idiomatic Rust, keeping the original semantics while adding stronger types and clearer module boundaries. It boots on emulated i386 in QEMU with processes, demand-paged virtual memory, CoW fork, the Minix v1 filesystem, and an 80+ coreutils userland, plus a one-command script to build a bootable disk image.

title: Zig creator calls Bun’s Claude Rust rewrite ‘unreviewed slop’
blurb: Zig creator Andrew Kelley publicly criticized Bun's Claude-assisted Rust rewrite as "unreviewed slop," arguing that shipping large volumes of AI-generated code without human review erodes maintainability. The dispute crystallizes a growing rift among systems maintainers over whether LLM-driven rewrites belong in foundational runtimes and toolchains that thousands of projects depend on.

title: React Native + Expo JWT Auth with FastAPI — A Complete Working Example
blurb: This walkthrough builds a production JWT auth flow for React Native + Expo against a FastAPI backend, covering token issuing with PyJWT and bcrypt, secure token storage in the iOS Keychain and Android Keystore via expo-secure-store, and automatic refresh through axios interceptors. The key hardening detail is a type claim on each token that prevents a refresh token from being replayed as an access token, keeping any stolen short-lived access token useful for only minutes.

title: TerraRepair: A Tool-Grounded LLM Agent for Infrastructure-as-Code Repair
blurb: TerraRepair is a tool-grounded LLM agent that repairs Terraform misconfigurations flagged by IaC scanners, retrieving dependency context and provider schemas and re-running the scanner before returning a fix, and escalating instead of fabricating when deployment-specific context is missing. On combined AWS benchmarks it raised scanner-verified fix rates from 26.6% to 78.4% on Checkov and 44.8% to 72.4% on Trivy over a one-shot baseline.

title: Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6-Sol: How to Orchestrate Claude and Codex to Ship More Reliable Code
blurb: OpenAI shipped an official plugin that installs inside Claude Code, letting Fable 5 orchestrate while Codex's GPT-5.6 writes the code and Fable reviews the actual diff before approving. The walkthrough covers installation, wiring up the /route command, and the two-model handoff, then examines why a lab would embed its engine in a rival's tool and where value in AI coding is shifting.

title: How I Eliminated 19,494 Duplicate MongoDB Records (And What I'd Do Differently Today)
blurb: A developer inherited a job platform where "United States" or "worldwide" listings silently expanded into one identical record per city, ballooning a single posting into 19,494 duplicates and pushing some endpoints past 7,000ms. The write-up traces the diagnosis through a schema-and-query checklist, walks through the deduplication cleanup, and reflects on the data-model changes that would have prevented the fan-out in the first place.

title: Explaining to business people why building software is still hard
blurb: The author argues that non-technical stakeholders misjudge software difficulty because the hard part isn't writing code, it's managing accumulating complexity, and LLM tools like Lovable make this worse by hiding it. Recounting a hackathon where an app went from 90% done to barely usable overnight, he shows how AI codegen accelerates the promising start while deferring the infra work and quality ownership that determine whether software actually holds together.

title: Fail-Aware and Explainable Test Oracle Prediction
blurb: This paper introduces FOCAL, a code LLM that predicts whether a test prefix passes or fails rather than generating assertions, using a loss that weights failing cases and grounding each call in statement-level behavioral evidence. Against the SEER baseline it sharply improves failure detection on unseen projects where prior discriminative oracles collapse, positioning it to attach fault-aware oracles to test prefixes produced by fuzzing or LLM-based generation.

title: ThinkLog: Leveraging Reasoning for Log Statement Generation
blurb: ThinkLog is an LLM-based method for end-to-end log statement generation that injects reasoning as few-shot examples to decide log placement, severity level, and message wording. Evaluated on 9,619 Java methods from public GitHub repos, it reaches 20.55% accuracy, a 15.4% gain over the prior best method, at roughly half the inference cost in dollars.

title: Complexity Theory of Randomised Testing
blurb: This paper builds complexity-theoretic foundations for randomised testing, modeling generators as Turing transducers that consume random bits and proving the theoretically generable languages coincide exactly with the recursively enumerable ones. It shows polynomial-time generation lies within NP but diverges from decision complexity, and proves no property-based testing library can compositionally derive efficient generators from predicates using conjunction or negation.

title: Why Vanilla JavaScript
blurb: The author argues that building web apps in plain JavaScript, without a framework, avoids the churn of dependency upgrades, build tooling, and abstraction layers that outlive their usefulness. The case rests on longevity and control: code written against browser APIs directly keeps working for years, while the trade-off is writing more of the plumbing that React or Vue would otherwise hand you.

title: Understanding the Odin programming language
blurb: Karl Zylinski's "Understanding the Odin Programming Language" teaches the language from procedures and manual memory management through parametric polymorphism and data-oriented design, aimed at anyone with prior programming experience. The book emphasizes why Odin makes its design choices rather than only how to write it, and version 1.10 swaps the deprecated Small_Array for the Fixed Capacity Dynamic Array.

title: Predicting Program Comprehension with Foundation Models of Human Cognition
blurb: This paper tests whether Centaur, a foundation model of human cognition trained on 160 general psychology experiments, can predict how developers comprehend code by evaluating it against 9 published program-comprehension studies. Centaur aligns with human response patterns better than its Llama 3.1 base, relies less on prior-trial data, and gains more from task context, suggesting behavioral patterns from general psychology transfer to modeling developer behavior.

title: Benchmarking 15 “E-Waste” GPUs with Modern Workloads
blurb: A developer benchmarked 15 decommissioned NVIDIA Tesla GPUs, including a 24GB K80 that sells for $60 and a V100-16GB under $200, to gauge whether idle enterprise VRAM is viable for homelab inference. The post covers building llama.cpp from source for EOL CUDA architectures like Kepler, a 4U node BOM on cheap X99 Xeon hardware, and the power-efficiency trade-off that only bites in always-on workloads.

title: Logseq 2.0 Beta (DB version) is here
blurb: Logseq released the first beta of Logseq 2.0, which moves the note-taking app from Markdown files to a database-backed storage engine. The DB version reworks how blocks and pages are stored to support faster queries and richer structured data, though it ships as an early beta with rough edges and requires backing up existing graphs before testing.

title: Ghostel.el: Terminal emulator powered by libghostty
blurb: Ghostel.el is an Emacs terminal emulator built on libghostty-vt, the same VT engine behind the Ghostty terminal, with a native Zig dynamic module handling terminal state, rendering, and PTY I/O while Elisp manages buffers, keymaps, and remote process integration. Following emacs-libvterm's two-layer design, it adds capabilities libvterm lacks, including the Kitty keyboard and graphics protocols, OSC 8 hyperlinks, rich underline styles, and synchronized output, with the native module downloaded automatically on first use.

title: TIL - Algolia Makes Creating an MCP Server Stupid Easy
blurb: Algolia's dashboard can now turn any search index into a hosted MCP server in a few clicks, exposing it to Claude, Cursor, and other agents.

title: Show HN: Jacquard, a programming language for AI-written, human-reviewed code
blurb: Jacquard is a research programming language for AI-written, human-reviewed code, shipping a v0.1 release candidate with an OCaml checker, a CPS interpreter, a C-emitting AOT backend, and a Warp test framework. Function signatures expose which effects code may perform, so the runtime rejects unhandled effects without explicit `--allow` authority and lets one program run against real, faked, recorded, or probabilistic worlds by swapping handlers.

title: Software Has Changed
blurb: The author argues that AI-assisted development has reshaped the job overnight, trading flow states for "thinking..." states and making experiments cheap enough to throw code away without a second thought. The upside is that good engineers now spend their freed-up energy reviewing, refactoring, and killing technical debt before it lands in main, and the open question is whether how code gets written matters once you've reviewed and trust the result.

title: Build agentic full-stack apps with Genkit
blurb: Genkit is an open-source framework for building full-stack, agentic AI apps across TypeScript, Go, Dart, and Python, now with a preview Agents API that packages message history, the tool loop, streaming, and persistence behind one interface. You define an agent on the server with a name and system prompt, then drive it through the same chat() API whether it runs in-process, streams a turn, or persists sessions via a pluggable store.

title: The four horsemen behind thousands of Postgres outages
blurb: The author, building pgrust as a reimplementation of Postgres, argues that a handful of well-known failure modes cause most production Postgres outages, especially on teams without a dedicated DBA. Chief among them is VACUUM: the background job that reclaims space from soft-deleted rows competes with query I/O when aggressive and falls behind when throttled, and if it lags on reclaiming 32-bit transaction IDs, Postgres shuts the database down to avoid wraparound corruption.

title: AutoSLO: Practical Latency SLOs on Cloud Data Warehouses -- Extended Version
blurb: AutoSLO is a workload-management framework for multi-cluster cloud warehouses that keeps queries within latency SLOs while cutting compute cost by a mean of 26.4% versus the next-best baseline on Redbench. It works across three timescales: a Policy Tuner that plans proactive scaling from forecasts, an SLO-aware Autoscaler that reacts to forecast drift, and a concurrency-aware Query Router that places live queries. The Autoscaler and Router alone cut SLO violation rates by 93.7% and 47.8% against their alternatives.

title: Modern Data Warehousing at Scale: Arcesium's Migration to DuckDB and Iceberg
blurb: Arcesium's engineering team documents how it rebuilt its data warehouse around DuckDB as the query engine and Iceberg as the table format, replacing a setup that struggled to scale for a firm handling large financial datasets. The write-up covers why decoupling storage from compute mattered, how Iceberg in object storage became the single source of truth, and where DuckDB's embedded engine handles analytical workloads without a heavyweight cluster. It is a concrete look at the storage-compute split most data teams are now weighing.

title: YouTrackDB is a general-use object-oriented graph database
blurb: JetBrains open-sourced YouTrackDB, an object-oriented graph database that traverses links in O(1) with no runtime JOINs and runs every transaction under snapshot isolation by default. It ships two query paths, Gremlin via the TinkerPop API and its own SQL-based YQL that uses dot notation for link traversal, and works in schema-less, schema-mixed, or schema-full modes with encryption at rest.

title: OptFSST: Optimized FSST String Compression
blurb: OptFSST reworks FSST string compression by replacing its greedy encoding with dynamic-programming-optimal encoding against a given symbol table, plus a frequency counter and pruning pass to build better tables. Across 92 real-world string datasets, it improves compression factors by an average 7.3% over FSST and 17.0% over FSST12 while keeping per-string random-access decompression, and OptFSST12 decompresses 1.2x faster than FSST12. The authors also prove the generalized symbol-table selection problem is NP-hard, which is why table construction stays heuristic.

title: Lakekeeper Generic Table API Design
blurb: Lakekeeper's Generic Table API, shipped in v0.13.0, lets a catalog govern non-Iceberg assets like Lance datasets without forcing them into an Iceberg schema. The author, who wrote the feature, frames it as a governance boundary rather than a query abstraction: the catalog owns identity, access, and lifecycle while each format's native reader handles the bytes. The takeaway is that treating table format and governance as separate concerns is mostly, and unsurprisingly, generic.

title: Re-architecting Affirm's Upfunnel Platform: How We Cut Experiment Cycle Time from Months to Days
blurb: Affirm migrated its upfunnel surface, the "As Low As $X/mo" messaging shown across its 500K+ merchant partnerships, out of a Python monolith into a standalone Kotlin microservice without touching a single merchant integration. Experiment setup dropped from roughly two months to four days, P99 latency fell about 47%, and a configuration table of hundreds of millions of mostly-unread rows collapsed into a rule engine of a few million. The write-up walks through migrating live traffic when the caller can't help you.

title: How Unity Catalog managed tables bring interoperability, performance, and unified governance to the Lakehouse
blurb: Databricks moved external access to Unity Catalog managed Delta tables into Public Preview, letting engines like Spark, Flink, Starburst, and DuckDB read and write a single governed copy of data. Managed tables now pair that multi-engine access with Predictive Optimization and catalog-coordinated commits, which Databricks says cut storage costs up to 50% and speed queries up to 20x versus external tables with no governance guarantees.

title: Beyond Redaction: Anatomy of a Privacy-Safe Data Platform
blurb: This piece argues that privacy engineering is about governing data in use, not hiding it, and that the right question for any privacy platform is which property a recipient must retain, for what permitted purpose, and what evidence proves the control ran. It maps controls to jobs: purpose-bound deterministic tokenization for approved internal joins, aggregation or clean-room workflows for external partners. A key caveat is that deterministic tokens are usually pseudonymous, not anonymous, so "PII-free" often overstates the guarantee.

title: ClickHouse on Docker Hardened Images
blurb: ClickHouse is now available as Docker Hardened Images, stripped-down builds of the server, ClickHouse Keeper, the Kubernetes Operator, its Helm chart, and the metrics exporter that ship only the packages each component needs to run. The database behaves identically, but dropping the Ubuntu base removes unused packages like wget and Perl that carry unpatched CVEs, so images clear Trivy, Grype, and ECR scans that block enterprise deployments.

title: We benchmarked coding agents on our own internal tasks at Databricks and learned a lot!
blurb: Databricks benchmarked coding agents against real internal engineering tasks instead of SWE-Bench, whose public numbers teams often over-tune for. Several open-source models are now top-tier, with GLM 5.2 competitive across a messy Scala, Go, Rust, and Protobuf codebase. The bigger lesson is economic: the simple Pi harness matched vendor harnesses at half the cost by sending smaller inputs, and cheaper-per-token models like Sonnet 5 burned more tokens, ending up pricier and worse than Opus 4.8.

title: The benchmark grading every text-to-SQL model has wrong answers in its key
blurb: sqlsure pointed a deterministic semantic checker at the gold queries behind BIRD and Spider, the benchmarks used to rank every text-to-SQL model, and found provable errors in the answer key. BIRD dev question #571 is off by 8x: its gold query fans out a votes-posts join into a cartesian product, returning 3.0 instead of the correct 0.375. A follow-up audit found 8.2% of joins in BIRD's 9,428 training queries unbacked by any declared key relationship.

title: Lightning Fast Matching Dependency Discovery with Desbordante
blurb: Desbordante, an open-source data profiler, ships a reworked implementation of HyMD, the state-of-the-art algorithm for discovering matching dependencies, the fuzzy generalization of functional dependencies that power entity resolution and deduplication. Three optimizations, a new sampling technique, faster generalization lookups, and a leaner dependency representation, deliver an average 40x speedup over the prior implementation and past 170x in some cases. It exposes bidirectional Python bindings, so teams can call the C++ core and supply their own similarity functions.

title: How I Eliminated 19,494 Duplicate MongoDB Records (And What I'd Do Differently Today)
blurb: A frontend dev handed full ownership of a job platform traced 7,000ms API response times to a MongoDB write pattern that fanned out "country" and "worldwide" job listings into one record per city, generating 19,494 duplicates that differed only by _id and location. The write-side denormalization made every read scan a bloated collection. The fix and the retrospective show why scope should live in a query filter, not in duplicated rows.

title: Show HN: I implemented a neural network in SQL
blurb: A developer implemented a full neural network, including forward and backward passes, in SQL, training it on MNIST through recursive queries and joins rather than any Python ML framework. The exercise reframes gradient descent as set-based relational operations, showing the warehouse can express far more than aggregation. It is a curiosity rather than a production pattern, but a useful reminder of how much compute already lives inside the query engine most data teams treat as read-only.

title: The Nuts and Bolts of Natural Language to SQL Translation: A Systematic Analysis of Model Pipeline Optimisation Approaches and their Interactions
blurb: This paper studies how to build lightweight NL2SQL pipelines by combining components like the NatSQL intermediate representation, synthetic-data fine-tuning, and a novel reranker, then measures each part's contribution with an ablation and Shapley analysis across the SmBoP and RASAT backbones. The key finding is that stacking every optimization doesn't yield the best model. Each extension's value depends on its interactions with the baseline and with the other components, which argues against treating pipeline tricks as independently additive.

title: The Data Anarchy Tax: Why your team is firefighting 45% of the time.
blurb: A June 2026 pulse survey of 212 data engineers found that while 85% of teams have a clear owner for infrastructure, 74.5% have no dedicated owner for the data products stakeholders actually use, and a quarter said their dashboards and models are pure anarchy. The author argues that this ownership gap, not legacy systems, is the real bottleneck, with "lack of leadership direction" and "poor requirements" driving teams to spend 45% of their time firefighting.

title: How Data Narratives Go Wrong: A Taxonomy of Issues Across the Data Communication Process
blurb: This paper introduces TIC, a Taxonomy of Issues in Data Communication, built from qualitative annotation of 700 real-world data narratives from fact-checking sites, research datasets, and controversial media. It organizes recurring failures across six dimensions - data, analysis, visual encoding, text, reasoning, and interpretation - and maps them onto the full pipeline from analysis to audience reception. The authors ship the taxonomy alongside an annotated case corpus and an interactive browser for diagnosing where a narrative breaks down.

title: Mesh-89 device lets Palestinian drivers stay ahead of checkpoint closures
blurb: Central Saint Martins graduate Joud Abuhomos designed Mesh-89, an off-grid device that alerts Palestinian bus drivers to the real-time status of the West Bank's 89 permanent checkpoints. The deliberately plain-looking unit uses mesh networking to route closure updates without cellular infrastructure, turning a graduate design project into a working tool for navigating daily movement restrictions.

title: Figma Acquires Team Behind a Vibe-coding App
blurb: Figma acquired the team behind Bud, the vibe-coding and AI agent platform formerly known as Orchids, folding another coding-and-prototyping layer into its canvas. Both products shut down by July 18, forcing users to migrate projects, as Figma pushes past static ideation toward tools that build and prototype working apps alongside Figma Make and its Codex and Claude Code integrations.

title: Taste cannot be delegated
blurb: The author argues that taste cannot be encoded or delegated, because both AI models and consensus-driven organizations are good at recognizing what is preferred but incapable of deciding what deserves to exist. The essay ties two earlier pieces together, making the case that a designer's value now lives in the willingness to own a decision, not in producing or aggregating more options.

title: How to build a digital painting from the ground up using Photoshop and Blender
blurb: Fantasy illustrator Pierre Ignaszewski walks through his process for a personal piece, blocking out architecture and mood in Blender before exporting to Photoshop to render the queen, creatures, and background front to back. The core discipline is legibility: establish global values, colors, and silhouettes first, reserve the widest value range and finest detail for the focal point, and simplify the background so it never competes.

title: Hovis and Kingsmill are now one business – and BrandOpus has given it an identity with no wheat in sight
blurb: Allied Bakeries completed its acquisition of Hovis Group, merging Hovis, Kingsmill, Allinson's and Sunblest into Hovis Bakeries, the UK's largest bread manufacturer at roughly 35% market share, with BrandOpus handling positioning and identity. The studio dropped the category's stock imagery of wheat fields, sliced loaves and sunrises for an "Elemental Power" system and a "Nourishing the Nation" line, betting on stature over nostalgia in a wrapped-bread market that has declined for over a decade.

title: How Studio Kiln built one 'particle' system to unify all four of BAFTA's awards
blurb: Studio Kiln unified BAFTA's four ceremonies (Film, Television, Games, Television Craft) under one identity built as a particle system in Cavalry, where flowing coloured particles gather into BAFTA's facets to trace a work's path from idea to finished piece. Rather than hand-animate each ceremony, the studio used shape and colour to differentiate the four worlds while keeping particle behaviour, motion, and type identical, so one adaptable framework generates motion, digital, and print assets across every format.

title: How Decagon Uses AI for Design System Saturation
blurb: Decagon documents how it scaled a new design system by pairing Figma MCP and Figma Make with agentic coding tools, building an org-wide library of hundreds of components that logged tens of thousands of inserts in 30 days. The core argument is that coding agents only produce consistent output when their inputs are consistent, so a well-structured design system becomes the single source of truth that keeps the design-to-code loop from breaking at speed.

title: Ragged Edge rebrands AI notepad Granola with a co-founder's handwriting and a deliberately imperfect logo
blurb: Ragged Edge rebranded AI meeting-notes app Granola around a co-founder's handwriting and an intentionally rough, napkin-scribbled logo, trading the usual productivity-app polish for a "progress over process" identity. The launch trended on X, drove Granola's biggest download day, and preceded a $125m raise at a $1.5bn valuation, making it the world's second-fastest-growing software brand that month per Ramp.

title: Design-System Maturity: A 6-Dimension Framework
blurb: Nielsen Norman Group argues that design-system maturity is not a linear climb but a multidimensional state, assessed across six axes spanning design, development, strategy, operations, governance, and organizational politics. The framework accounts for what ladder models miss: systems that regress after mergers, maturity that looks different at a 10-person startup versus a 10,000-person enterprise, and adoption that stays an ongoing fight at every stage.

title: Neri&Hu wraps Hong Kong hotel in "urban tapestry" facade
blurb: Neri&Hu redesigned the facade of Mandarin Oriental The Landmark in Hong Kong's Central district, using staggered terracotta tiles that read as a piece of woven tapestry against Queen's Road. The work pairs with Joyce Wang Studio's interior renovation, giving the hotel a textured, material-forward exterior that trades glass-tower anonymity for something closer to civic craft.

title: Making Images Accessible
blurb: Every `` element must carry an alt attribute per the HTML spec, but the decision that actually matters is whether the image is informative (needs a description tied to WCAG 1.1.1) or decorative (needs an empty alt, or belongs in CSS entirely). The post's practical test is a single question: if the image disappeared, would the user lose information, with icons treated as a special case since they usually just reinforce adjacent text.

title: Who made this, Designing excuses, DesignOps in the age of AI
blurb:
This is a curated weekly digest, so the blurb should capture its throughline.

Fabricio Teixeira's latest UX Collective roundup circles a single question raised by generative tools: "Who made this?" and what audiences still trust once process becomes invisible. The curated pieces extend the theme into practice, from design engineering's shifting boundaries to the argument that understanding, not execution, is now the real bottleneck.

title: Rethinking Cheapie Inline Lamp Switches
blurb: Industrial designer Andrew King's self-directed Modal project redesigns the inline lamp switch, the plastic afterthought common on cheap lamps, as a CNC-machined aluminum housing with a low rounded form and a flush latching push-button. The writeup details how manufacturing and use drove the form, from internal guides that kill side-to-side play to iterated button travel and proportions, finished in bead-blasted anodized clear, black, or blue.

title: Designing for Trust
blurb: The author argues that trust, not industry vertical, is the real through-line of design work, drawing on pediatric telehealth, wealth management, and voting products that share nothing on the surface but ask users to hand over something they can't afford to lose. Trust lives in framing, copy confidence, and the small moments a product reassures before being asked, and a single confusing screen collapses it for the session.

title: Lessons Learned from CISA’s Recent GitHub Leak
blurb: CISA published a postmortem on a contractor who left 844 MB of internal data, including administrative AWS GovCloud keys and plaintext credentials for dozens of systems, in a public GitHub repo for nearly six months. The agency took over 48 hours to rotate the exposed secrets, citing system complexity, and admitted its lack of clear reporting channels forced the researcher to escalate through the contractor, its VDP, and eventually a journalist. The lessons: maintain tested key-rotation capabilities and publish distinct intake channels for incidents affecting your own organization.

title: Cavern Manticore: Exposing Iran-Linked Modular C2 Framework
blurb: Check Point Research linked a new modular .NET command-and-control framework, dubbed Cavern, to Cavern Manticore, an Iran MOIS-nexus APT tied to OilRig's Lyceum subgroup that targets Israeli IT providers and government bodies. The operators abused SysAid's legitimate software-deployment feature (no SysAid vulnerability was involved) to sideload the Cavern Agent via a trojanized WinDirStat DLL, and compile components into three .NET binary formats to force analysts across multiple reverse-engineering toolsets.

title: Microsoft Maps Three Salesforce Attack Paths Tied to a Year of ShinyHunters Activity
blurb: Microsoft mapped a year of Salesforce data-extortion activity, attributed to ShinyHunters (UNC6040/UNC6240), to three abuse paths that exploit no platform flaw: vishing employees into approving malicious OAuth connected apps, stolen OAuth tokens from compromised vendors, and misconfigured guest access. Because the access rides trusted connected apps and real user consent, authentication logs barely register it, so Microsoft and Salesforce shipped new detection and OAuth governance tooling to surface post-consent app behavior.

title: When checking the URL isn't enough: a Device Code Phishing attack via a Microsoft website
blurb: Kaspersky's Securelist documented an active Device Code Phishing campaign that abuses Microsoft's OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant flow, tricking victims into entering an attacker-generated user_code on the legitimate login.microsoftonline.com page, which yields the attacker a valid access token. Because the credential entry happens on a trusted Microsoft domain, URL-inspection advice fails; defenders should restrict the device code flow via Conditional Access and alert on unexpected devicecode authentications.

title: Researchers detail "context bombing", where defenders use prompt injections to trigger guardrails of attackers' LLMs, cutting AI hacking success rates by ~90% (Dan Goodin/Ars Technica)
blurb: Researchers detailed "context bombing," a defensive technique that plants prompt injections in web content and files to trip the safety guardrails of attackers' LLMs, refusing malicious requests mid-operation. In testing, the method cut AI-driven hacking success rates by roughly 90%, turning agentic attackers' own alignment training into a mitigation defenders can seed across likely targets.

title: Grok Build Uploads Entire Git Repositories to xAI Storage, Not Just Files It Reads
blurb: Researcher cereblab found that xAI's Grok Build coding CLI (v0.2.93) uploads entire Git repositories, full commit history included, to an xAI-controlled GCS bucket named grok-code-session-traces, not just the files a task touches. Captures showed a 12 GB repo moving 5.10 GiB over a separate /v1/storage channel and recovered an unread canary file plus an unredacted tracked .env; disabling "Improve the model" did not stop the upload.

title: Compromised Injective SDK npm Package Exfiltrates Wallet Keys and Mnemonics
blurb: Socket detected a malicious @injectivelabs/sdk-ts@1.20.21 npm release that records wallet private keys and mnemonic phrases through fake telemetry before exfiltrating them, and the compromise spread to 17 additional @injectivelabs-scoped packages that pinned the poisoned version, exposing transitive users. The package pulls roughly 50,000 weekly downloads and shipped via a trusted maintainer's compromised GitHub account; it has been deprecated but not yet removed from npm.

title: Securing the AI supply chain on GKE: Introducing k8s-aibom for automated AI BOMs
blurb: Google open-sourced k8s-aibom, an unprivileged Kubernetes controller that continuously watches the cluster API to detect running AI runtimes like vLLM, Triton, and TGI and generate OWASP CycloneDX 1.6 ML-BOMs. It deploys as a single unprivileged Deployment with no sidecars, eBPF, or privileged DaemonSets, giving security teams runtime visibility into shadow AI workloads that evade traditional scanners.

title: The US government warns that Russia state hackers are coming after your router
blurb: CISA and allied agencies from Australia, Denmark, New Zealand, and the UK warned that Russian FSB Center 16 actors (Berserk Bear, Energetic Bear, Static Tundra) are mass-compromising home and small-office routers to proxy operations against critical infrastructure. The primary vector is scanning for SNMP agents with default or common credentials, then using spoofed traffic to run malware, so operators should disable internet-facing SNMP and rotate default community strings.

title: What xAI's Grok build CLI sends to xAI: A wire-level analysis
blurb: A wire-level teardown of xAI's official Grok Build CLI (grok) documents that, on a normal consumer login, it transmits file contents verbatim — including a .env secrets file — to xAI via POST /v1/responses and POST /v1/storage, and uploads the entire repository as a git bundle independent of what the agent reads, recovering a file it was told not to open. Captures land in the GCS bucket grok-code-session-traces, are on by default, and persist after disabling "Improve the model.

title: CISA warns of actively exploited RCE flaws in Joomla extensions
blurb: CISA added two Joomla extension flaws to its KEV catalog, CVE-2026-48939 in iCagenda and CVE-2026-56291 in Balbooa Forms, both arbitrary file upload bugs that allow attackers to upload PHP web shells and achieve RCE for full site takeover. Both were exploited in automated attacks before patches shipped, with the Balbooa Forms flaw hit as a zero-day since July 8; administrators should update to iCagenda 4.0.8 or Balbooa Forms 2.4.1 immediately.

title: Google and Microsoft Pull ModHeader With 1.6 Million Installs After Dormant Collector Found
blurb: Google and Microsoft pulled ModHeader, a header-editing extension with roughly 1.6 million Chrome and Edge installs, after Stripe OLT found a dormant browsing-history collector shipped inside the signed store build, gated behind an empty allow-list. The pipeline already carries a hardcoded key, fingerprinting, and a daily beacon to api.stanfordstudies[.]com, so populating that list to activate collection would take only a routine update with no new permissions.

title: RabbitMQ Vulnerability Threatens Enterprise Systems
blurb: RabbitMQ contains an unauthenticated OAuth secret disclosure (CVE-2026-57219, CVSS 8.7) in an obsolete management-interface endpoint, letting anyone who reaches the management port fetch the broker's confidential client secret and impersonate it to the identity provider for an admin token. It affects deployments using an OAuth2/OIDC client secret; patch to 4.3.0, 4.2.6, 4.1.11, 4.0.20, or 3.13.15, restrict the management port, and rotate the secret.

title: Rethinking MCP Security: A Large-Scale Study of Runtime MCP Servers and Security Scanner Reliability
blurb: A large-scale study introduces MCPZoo, a runtime collection of 64,611 unique MCP servers, and uses it to test the reliability of MCP security scanners at ecosystem scale. Although existing scanners flag 96.89% of servers as risky, manual validation found fewer than 50% of sampled alerts were true positives and scanner outputs disagreed with one another, indicating current MCP scanning practices overstate exposure and shouldn't be trusted for triage.

title: Bonzo Lend Loses $9M on Hedera in Supra Oracle Exploit
blurb: Bonzo Lend, a lending protocol on Hedera, lost roughly $9.05M on July 11 after an attacker exploited a verification flaw in a third-party Supra oracle that accepted a degenerate BLS signature and zero-valued public key on the SAUCE/wHBAR feed. Using a few dollars of SAUCE collateral and a manipulated price, the attacker borrowed 6.63M USDC and 34.52M wrapped HBAR; over $5.25M was bridged to Ethereum and swapped into ETH within hours.

title: Misconfigured Server Reveals Three Evilginx Phishing Operations Targeting Microsoft 365
blurb: French security firm Lexfo mapped three Microsoft 365 phishing operations after one operator left directory listing enabled on a live attack server (185.163.204[.]7, Budapest), exposing phishing configs, credential logs, combolists, and Telegram session files that pivoted investigators to two more operators. All three run custom Evilginx AiTM forks but defeat MFA two mechanically distinct ways, one proxying the live login and one abusing a legitimate Microsoft sign-in flow, so M365 defenders need separate mitigations for each.

title: iCagenda and Balbooa Forms Joomla Flaws Reportedly Exploited as Zero-Days
blurb: **CISA added two maximum-severity Joomla extension flaws, CVE-2026-48939 in iCagenda and CVE-2026-56291 in Balbooa Forms (both CVSS 10.0), to its KEV catalog after zero-day exploitation enabling unauthenticated file upload and remote code execution.** Patches shipped in iCagenda 4.0.8/3.9.15 and Balbooa Forms 2.4.1; site owners should hunt for planted PHP shells in the attachments and uploads folders.

title: 148 npm Packages Disguised as Student Proxies Turned Browsers Into a DDoS Botnet
blurb: JFrog found 148 npm packages disguised as student web proxies that turned visitors' browsers into a Wisp-protocol DDoS botnet for roughly two weeks in May, using the registry as free hosting rather than targeting developers at install time. The proxy sites carried no lifecycle hooks or build scripts but loaded a swappable remote code loader and WebSocket flood generator, conscripting any student who opened a page into the swarm.

title: Forg365 PhaaS Targets Microsoft 365 with Device Code and AitM Session Theft
blurb: Forg365, a new phishing-as-a-service kit sold on Telegram for $400/month, targets Microsoft 365 with device-code phishing and AitM session theft, using Amazon SES and SendGrid to blend lures into legitimate email traffic. Its device-auth branch pushes victims through a real Microsoft Authentication Broker flow so the authorized session lands with the attacker, defeating standard MFA.

title: Attacker Uses Suspected AI-Generated PowerShell Script to Map Active Directory
blurb: Huntress attributed a June 2026 intrusion to an attacker who ran a suspected AI-generated PowerShell script for Active Directory enumeration, entering via RDP with pre-compromised credentials and staging tools in C:\ProgramData. The script harvested users, computers, groups, OUs, and trusts into CSVs before exfiltration, with telltale LLM signs like the title "100% Working AD Information Gathering Script - FULLY FIXED" and noisy, over-engineered fallback logic.

title: Hackers backdoor Jscrambler npm package with infostealer malware
blurb: A threat actor published a malicious version of Jscrambler's npm package using compromised publishing credentials, embedding an infostealer in the preinstall hook that harvested developer secrets, cloud credentials, MCP configs, and crypto wallets across releases 8.14, 8.16, 8.17, and 8.20. The package was downloaded 1,479 times during a two-hour window before deprecation; teams that installed it should rotate Git, SSH, CI/CD, and cloud credentials and upgrade to the clean 8.22 release.

title: Introducing Precursor: detecting agentic behavior with continuous client-side signals
blurb: Cloudflare launched Precursor, a client-side, session-based verification system that injects JavaScript to continuously collect behavioral signals and distinguish human traffic from automated or agentic traffic in real time. An optional complement to Turnstile within Enterprise Bot Management, it scores behavior across the full user journey rather than a single checkpoint, catching automation that passes individual CAPTCHAs but cannot sustain consistent human behavior over a session.

title: New CrashStealer malware poses as Apple crash reporting tool
blurb: CrashStealer, a new macOS infostealer tracked by Jamf, impersonates Apple's CrashReporter.app and ships via an Apple-notarized installer ("Werkbit Setup") that bypasses Gatekeeper without warnings, then displays a fake password prompt to unlock the Keychain. Observed in active attacks since early July, it drains browser credentials, Keychain secrets, 14 password managers, and 80-plus crypto wallet extensions, so revoke the abused notarization certificate and hunt for the com.apple.crashreporter.helper LaunchAgent.

title: Now, defenders are embracing the prompt injection, too
blurb: Researchers at Tracebit disclosed "context bombing," a defensive technique that plants prompt injections inside decoy AWS secrets to trigger refusal mechanisms in attacking LLM agents, causing them to shut down when they enumerate the planted strings. Across 152 runs against five models, a single decoy string cut full-account admin takeover from 57% to 5% and persistent compromise from 36% to 1%; Opus 4.8 dropped from admin access in 93% of runs to zero. Defenders can seed honeytokens with forbidden-content prompts to disrupt autonomous AI intrusions.

title: US and allies warn of Russian critical infrastructure attacks
blurb: The NSA, FBI, CISA, and 15 allied agencies issued a joint advisory attributing a campaign against critical infrastructure routers to Russia's FSB Center 16 (Berserk Bear, Static Tundra), which scans for default or weak SNMP strings and exploits Cisco Smart Install via CVE-2018-0171 to exfiltrate device configs over TFTP. Defenders in energy, communications, and government should upgrade to SNMPv3, disable Smart Install, block TFTP and SNMP at edge firewalls, and replace end-of-life devices.

title: Multiple Jscrambler Packages Impacted by Supply Chain Attack
blurb: A threat actor used stolen NPM publishing credentials to push malicious Jscrambler package versions (8.16–8.20) starting July 11, each carrying a preinstall hook that drops Rust infostealer binaries for Linux, macOS, and Windows. The malware harvests credentials, cloud secrets, crypto wallets, and MCP configs; upgrade to clean version 8.22 and rotate any secrets exposed on affected build hosts.

title: Unauthenticated RCE in Motorola's MR2600 Router
blurb: Researcher MrBruh disclosed an unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in Motorola's MR2600 Wi-Fi 5 router, chaining two firmware-update endpoints that were meant to require authentication but didn't. The upload handler validates a SEAMA image by checking the multipart body's first four bytes for the magic header, so an attacker who sends the raw firmware without multipart boundaries bypasses the check and flashes malicious firmware. The last firmware, v1.0.22, shipped in mid-2024.

title: Linux disk encryption and self-encrypting drives -- A case study on Opal2 drives security
blurb: Researchers black-box tested 38 commercial Opal2 self-encrypting drives from multiple vendors and found several firmware security flaws and Linux integration incompatibilities, all responsibly disclosed to the affected vendors. The findings drove fixes in the disk encryption tooling shipped across all major Linux distributions, and the team released an open-source test suite so operators can independently evaluate their own Opal2 hardware.

title: When Local Monitors Miss Compositional Harm: Diagnosing Distributed Backdoors in Multi-Agent Systems
blurb: This paper shows that per-message runtime monitors in multi-agent LLM systems have a structural blind spot: a distributed backdoor splits a harmful payload across agents so every local check passes while the assembled object is the attack. The authors prove that once fragments look benign in the monitored view, no detector on that view can catch them. Detection returns only when the monitor evaluates the reassembled object, so defenders should monitor composed artifacts, not just individual steps.

title: Introducing GuardDog 3.0: A new rules engine, transparent sandboxing, and more
blurb: Datadog released GuardDog 3.0, an open-source scanner for malicious PyPI, npm, and Go packages that replaces its Semgrep source-code heuristics with YARA rules run via yara-python for faster, lower-memory scanning at scale. The release adds a risk-scoring engine that correlates weak heuristic signals (unusual metadata, install-script execution vectors, GitHub-to-registry manifest mismatches) into a confidence-weighted maliciousness verdict rather than emitting one unprioritized finding per rule match.

title: Agent Hacks Agent: Autoresearch for Production-Agent Red-Teaming
blurb: This paper introduces AHA, an automated red-teaming loop that discovers reusable vulnerabilities in production LLM agents like Claude Code and Codex by having one agent probe another. AHA proposes a vulnerability hypothesis, builds a falsifier, runs the attack in a sandbox, and promotes confirmed findings into a Vulnerability Concept Graph. The frozen graph beats the strongest discovery baseline by 14.2 points single-shot and gives safety teams an auditable artifact to inspect flaws and validate patches.

title: RedHook Android malware now uses Wireless ADB for shell access
blurb: A new RedHook Android banking trojan variant, analyzed by Group-IB, abuses Wireless ADB to gain shell-level (UID 2000) privileges without a rooted device or a computer connection. After tricking victims into granting Accessibility permissions, it enables Wireless Debugging, self-pairs via the loopback interface, and uses a Shizuku-based framework to run 53 server-issued commands including screen streaming, keylogging, credential theft, and silent app installation.

title: Graph-Based Structural Evaluation of LLM-Translated Adversary Emulation Procedures
blurb: This paper introduces Graph-Based Structural Evaluation (GBSE), a method that models each adversary emulation procedure as a directed attributed graph and scores LLM cross-platform translations by normalized Graph Edit Distance across technique, tactic, telemetry, and Sigma logsource layers. Applied to a 29-step ALPHV/BlackCat Windows-to-Linux plan, it caught structural drift that binary scoring misses: technique and tactic structure were fully preserved, but telemetry similarity fell to 0.897 and technical realism scored 0.43, keeping the plan below the 0.80 deployment threshold.

title: U.S. Sanctions First VPN Service and Malware Cryptor Seller Over Ransomware Support
blurb: OFAC sanctioned VPN provider First VPN Service (1VPNS), its Ukrainian administrator Dmytro Rashevskyi, and Belarusian cryptor seller Yegeniy Silayev for enabling ransomware operators to obscure attack origins and evade detection against U.S. hospitals, financial firms, and municipal governments. The no-logs 1VPNS, live since 2014 and dismantled in a joint EU-North American operation in May 2026, tied ransomware groups to billions in losses across American businesses and critical infrastructure.

title: CrashStealer macOS Malware Uses Notarized Dropper to Pass Gatekeeper Checks
blurb: CrashStealer, a native C++ macOS information stealer flagged by Jamf Threat Labs, harvests credentials, crypto wallets, password-manager data, and keychain material, encrypting the haul with AES-GCM before exfiltrating over libcurl. It ships inside an Apple-notarized dropper ("Werkbit.app") signed with a valid developer ID, letting it pass Gatekeeper; defenders should revoke the "Emil Grigorov (WWB7JA7AQV)" signing identity and block the werkbit[.]io domain.

title: Japan's largest taxi operator shuts systems after cyberattack
blurb: Nihon Kotsu, Japan's largest taxi and chauffeur operator with roughly $1B in annual revenue and 8,558 vehicles, disconnected internal systems after detecting a malware-based intrusion early Saturday. The taxi dispatch, web booking, and reservation systems remain offline, no ransomware group has claimed responsibility, and the company has not yet confirmed whether data was exfiltrated.

title: EU and UK officially blame Russian spies for cyberattack on Poland's power grid
blurb: The EU and UK formally attributed a cyberattack on Poland's power grid to Russian state-backed operators, marking the first coordinated Western attribution of a Russian intrusion into an EU member's energy infrastructure. The joint statement escalates diplomatic pressure without naming specific sanctions, and utilities across the bloc should treat grid-facing OT systems as active targets for the same actors.

title: Progress orders emergency ShareFile server shutdown over mystery security threat
blurb: Progress Software ordered customers to shut down on-prem ShareFile storage servers after detecting an unspecified security threat serious enough to warrant an emergency out-of-band advisory, though the vendor has not disclosed a CVE, attack vector, or whether exploitation is active. Teams running self-managed ShareFile should take affected servers offline per the advisory, treat exposed instances as potentially compromised, and watch for follow-up detail given Progress's MOVEit and prior ShareFile track record.

title: Europe strikes out against Russia’s Turla over espionage, ‘destructive attacks’
blurb: The EU, its member states, and the UK sanctioned dozens of Russian individuals and entities over a Turla (Secret Blizzard) espionage campaign running since 2010 and FSB Center 16-directed attacks, including December's strike on Poland's energy grid that cut heat to 500,000 people. The UK's first EU-coordinated cyber sanctions named GRU leaders and operators behind Lumma Stealer, signaling a push to unmask the proxy networks Moscow uses to obscure state attribution.

title: Microsoft Entra ID authentication overhaul to start in September 2026
blurb: Microsoft will make passkeys the default authentication experience for Entra ID in the public cloud starting September 1, 2026, auto-enabling passkey registration for tenants that use SMS or voice MFA. From February 1, 2027, SMS and voice users must register a passkey to sign in, with no tenant opt-out, and Microsoft will retire native SMS and voice delivery entirely. Organizations still requiring telecom-based MFA for regulatory reasons must contract third-party providers through the Microsoft Security Store and absorb the costs.

title: Distributed Denial of Science: How Indirect Data Poisoning of AI Systems Can Industrialize Scientific Fraud
blurb: This paper introduces indirect data poisoning, where an adversary corrupts an open dataset and uploads the tainted variant to a public repository so that autonomous research agents retrieve it and propagate fraudulent conclusions. Tested across Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3.1 Pro over 450 runs, poisoning succeeded 49.56% of the time with only 6% detection, though a five-check data provenance audit during retrieval dropped attack success to zero.

title: Since Chromium 148, Math.tanh is now fingerprintable to link underlying OS
blurb: Since Chromium 148, browsers leak the host OS through floating-point math bits. `Math.tanh`, CSS trig functions, and the Web Audio compressor all route through the platform libm, and libraries like glibc, Apple's libsystem_m, and Windows UCRT round certain inputs differently by one ULP. A single `Math.tanh(0.8)` call now returns per-OS signatures that contradict a spoofed User-Agent, so anti-bot systems and defenders can flag automation whose math bits disagree with its claimed platform.

title: Organizations Warned of Exploited Joomla Extension Vulnerabilities
blurb: Two Joomla extension flaws, Balbooa Forms (CVE-2026-56291, CVSS 10) and iCagenda (CVE-2026-48939, CVSS 10), are being exploited in the wild as zero-days, both unauthenticated arbitrary file uploads that let attackers upload PHP and achieve RCE. Patches shipped in Balbooa Forms 2.4.1 and iCagenda 4.0.8/3.9.15, and CISA added both to its KEV catalog on July 10, so administrators should patch immediately and hunt for uploaded webshells.

title: Lidl discloses online shop breach after service provider hack
blurb: Lidl disclosed that attackers stole online shop customer data through a breach at a third-party service provider, affecting customers in Germany, Belgium, and the Netherlands. Exposed fields include names, phone numbers, email addresses, dates of birth, and customer numbers, but Lidl says passwords, payment details, and addresses were not compromised; affected users should watch for phishing that leverages the stolen PII.

title: GigaWiper Lets Threat Actors Choose Their Own Destructive Attack
blurb: Microsoft Threat Intelligence disclosed GigaWiper, a modular Golang implant that pairs full C2 and backdoor access with on-demand destructive payloads including disk wiping, fake ransomware, and system-level sabotage.
